body:
Ok I have managed to narrow down the issue, so for anyone having a similar problem, maybe this is it.
I was using an axios interceptor to add an authorization header (I tried the interceptor approach because I thought that maybe I'd have to do more processing down the line). The way I did it was incorrect: basically I replaced the `headers` of specific requests with the authorization headers, instead of just adding the authorization header.

Beginner mistake I suppose.
Anyway, better way to do the auth part, if you are only talking to one domain, is to use `axios.defaults.headers.common['Authorization'] = AUTH_TOKEN;`

body: `AxiosResponse` is also affected by the introduced breaking change as it has `config: AxiosRequestConfig<D>` (see [here in code](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.2.3/index.d.ts#L359)).
author: VGalata

body: The PR #5420 ontroduced a breaking API change in a patch versin release 1.2.3. This also broke [our pipeline](https://github.com/SAP/cloud-sdk-js/actions/runs/3944517822/jobs/6750513193). As mentioned replacing `AxiosRequestConfig` with `RawAxiosRequestConfig` solves the issue. However, it would be great if you could be more careful in the future. If you look at [the changes](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5420/files#) adding a mandatory field `headers` is obviously not type compatible in structural typing. So you should use assertions/checks in version 1.X.Y and make it mandatory in version 2.X.Y.

body: That's another thing we should think about. Why make everyone change from `AxiosRequestConfig` to `RawAxiosRequestConfig`, axios should've thought to creating another type like `FullAxiosRequestConfig` to have mandatory headers and kept `AxiosRequestConfig` for everyone.
author: arthurfiorette

body:
> type like FullAxiosRequestConfig

@arthurfiorette This was the second solution considered, however, it doesn't really fit in with the current naming convention where the `Raw` prefix is added to indicate untouched user input. Having something like `InternalAxiosConfig` is better for compatibility, but will likely lead to type-naming confusion. In any case, we need to somehow separate the raw config type provided by the user from the internal config representation, because in the future it will become even more difficult to do. Probably there is no absolutely perfect solution here, so we are open to suggestions.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body: @DigitalBrainJS  May I ask when the confirmed scheme will be available? Shall we change it to RawAxiosRequestConfig or fix this problem in the next version
author: laterdayi

body: regardless of what the best way to address the internal/external config going forward, surely there's realization that the harm done by 1.2.3 far outweighs the benefit and 1.2.3 should be "pulled".  Patch releases simply should not break builds.
author: jameyg42
rcoundon

body: @jameyg42 Definitely should, however, it is not always possible to make a fix for one thing without any impact on another. It seems that no matter what solution we choose here, the consequences cannot be completely avoided. The only question is where they will appear and how significantly they will affect.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
What about the prefix `Custom`?

To me, `Raw` sounds like some type that is used internally by Axios and has not been sanitized/transformed to make it usable by others.
author: bombillazo

body:
So it seems there are three options:

1) leave the update as is:
  External (user) config - `RawAxiosConfig`
  Internal - `AxiosConfig`
 2) partial rollback with an alias (probably until the next major release):
  External (user) config - `AxiosConfig` = `RawAxiosConfig` (alias)
  Internal - `InternalAxiosConfig`
3) partial rollback forever
  External (user) config - `AxiosConfig`
  Internal - `InternalAxiosConfig`

Any suggestions are welcome.
author: DigitalBrainJS

author: DigitalBrainJS

body: @arthurfiorette If we are still going to rename the interface in the next major version, then the 2nd option could be preferable since users who have already changed the interface's name will not be forced to rename it back. I don't think they'll be thrilled about it :) Of course, it depends on whether users use the updated AxiosConfig interface somewhere as an internal config or not. We need to think carefully about this step.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
> @rcoundon that was another issue due to a missing generic parameter in the interface declaration. This has been fixed in the codebase with #5478.

author: rcoundon

body: I believe **option 2** is the best approach at this point. We'll keep the fix introduced by #5420 to solve issue #5415 while leaving the interface name as before. Still a (relatively) small breaking change because of the required prop, but users won't have to change `AxiosConfig` to `RawAxiosConfig` everywhere. It seems like a good compromise between all circumstances to me 🙂

body:
Hotfix
If you ever get this error, the hotfix is to follow these steps:

delete node-modules folder
run command npm cache clean --force
run command npm install
install the package again with npm install your-package-name
It should all work fine after that.

If these commands still don’t solve your issue, and you use git as a source control system, you can try the unbeatable git clean -fdx command. After that, run npm install again. Beware – it removes all files not checked in to git. It may also remove your IDE settings etc., so use with care.

But it’s only a hotfix, a solution for now to unblock you.

body:
Can you make a successful authorization request with Postman? I suspect their Auth API should use `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` rather than `multipart/form-data` since the request does not contain binary data.

```js
const {data} = await oAuthClient.post(url, {
  'client_id': clientId,
  'client_secret': clientSecret,
  'grant_type': 'client_credentials',
  'scope': scope
}, {
  headers: {
    'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'
  }
});
```

From their docs (https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/v2-oauth2-auth-code-flow#protocol-details):

```
// Line breaks for legibility only

POST /{tenant}/oauth2/v2.0/token HTTP/1.1
Host: https://login.microsoftonline.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded    // <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<

client_id=6731de76-14a6-49ae-97bc-6eba6914391e
&scope=https%3A%2F%2Fgraph.microsoft.com%2Fmail.read
&code=OAAABAAAAiL9Kn2Z27UubvWFPbm0gLWQJVzCTE9UkP3pSx1aXxUjq3n8b2JRLk4OxVXr...
&redirect_uri=http%3A%2F%2Flocalhost%2Fmyapp%2F
&grant_type=authorization_code
&code_verifier=ThisIsntRandomButItNeedsToBe43CharactersLong 
&client_secret=JqQX2PNo9bpM0uEihUPzyrh    // NOTE: Only required for web apps. This secret needs to be URL-Encoded.
```

hi @vaidotasstrazdas this is not related to the browser "edge", This is something different, its a runtime that serverless functions can run on, you can read more on [vercel edge runtime](https://vercel.com/docs/concepts/functions/edge-functions/edge-runtime).

body:
Default adapter setting: `{adapter: ['xhr', 'http']}`
Axios is trying to use the first adapter available, so if you're getting this error it means your environment doesn't support both `xhr` and `http` adapters. Currently, the Axios adapter loader is fairly minimalistic, as a decent implementation requires other depend tasks to be done beforehand, so it can only display the latest adapter load error.
If you set `{adapter: 'xhr'}` you will get an error about `xhr` only. In practice, this means `XMLHttpRequest` API is not available.

body:
Ok here's what I have found.

The httpAdapter is imported at the top of the module.
This is a conditional export from the http.js module in the same directory

```
export default isHttpAdapterSupported && function httpAdapter(config) {
...
````
The boolean isHttpAdapterSupported is defined as

```
const isHttpAdapterSupported = typeof process !== 'undefined' && utils.kindOf(process) === 'process';
```
Which looks to me like it is trying to identify if this is running in Node rather than in a Browser.

Presumably you can add an http adapter to a Node project and use this in lieu of the one you would have used if you were in the browser.

I think the variable name is misleading and should be changed to something like "isBrowserEnv" or something and the logic inverted accordingly.

So if the http adapter is not loaded in the knownAdaptors and you specify to use it with your configuration, then it will return null.

The question is, as you say whether the error message is misleading. I would say possibly. In this case the adapter is not available in the build or the environment. 

The use case I think is invalid, because you seem to be doing this from within a Browser (Edge) so you shouldn't need to specify an adapter as you already have an http module loaded as part of the V8 engine.

If you wanted to specifiy an adaptor you could do so in your config by specifying a function that returns a Promise. See [here](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/5523)
author: suityou01

body: The problem of this adapter loading logic is, that it will fail when in a simulated environment, e.g. Jest. Jest simulates a browser environment, but needs to use the http-adapter of axios, because running in Node.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
>  when i set "adapter: 'http'" in the request config options with chrome browser, the console tells me: Error: Adapter 'http' is not available in the build.

That's how it should be. The http adapter code is physically removed from the browser bundle as it relies on node API & modules that don't exist in the browser, otherwise, this will lead to a build error, or will force the user to import node shims, which will add a lot of useless garbage to the bundle. Using the bundle settings, you can also remove any adapter from the build, for example, leaving only fetch and removing xhr. So the error message is quite relevant.

`undefined` -> unknown adapter
`null` -> known adapter, but the adapter was removed from the build with bundler (its module replaced with `null` - module)
`false` -> adapter is in the build, but is not supported (lack of required API in the environment, xhr and fetch can return this state).

author: davux

body:
Closing, this is by design. axios (and the underlying Node http/follow-redirects stack) only follows redirects for http: and https:. Custom schemes (myapp://, etc.) are not valid HTTP redirect targets and will surface as a network error.

If you need to intercept custom-scheme redirects, set maxRedirects: 0 and handle the 3xx response + Location header yourself.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
> Closing, this is by design. axios (and the underlying Node http/follow-redirects stack) only follows redirects for http: and https:. Custom schemes (myapp://, etc.) are not valid HTTP redirect targets and will surface as a network error.
> 
> If you need to intercept custom-scheme redirects, set maxRedirects: 0 and handle the 3xx response + Location header yourself.

Hi @jasonsaayman,

If I remember correctly, this issue is that in react-native this is not possible, because the client crash and gives the error and we are not able to handle it with the max redirect and the 300
author: sebastienlabine

body:
Thanks for the fast follow-up here @sebastienlabine you are right.

On React Native, axios uses the XHR adapter, and maxRedirects is a Node-only option. XHR exposes no API to disable or intercept redirects, so the underlying native stack (NSURLSession on iOS, OkHttp on Android) auto-follows the 302, then fails when it tries to dispatch smartbills:// as an HTTP target. That failure happens below axios and surfaces as a generic network error. There is nothing axios can do at that layer.

Options on the RN side:

- Change the server contract. Return 200 with the target URL in the body and let the client call `Linking.openURL(...)`.
- fetch with redirect: 'manual', the support for this is inconsistent across RN versions/platforms, verify before relying on it.

Do any of those work? If so we may need to update the docs

body:
I've encountered the same bug and after come investigation I discovered that inside the interceptor the headers (according to Typescript at least) might be undefined. This is incompatible with the type `AxiosRequestHeaders`, but since I don't know why TS says that the headers might be undefined, I see type assertion as the only solution.

The least safe option is to assert the whole request:
```typescript
return request as AxiosRequestConfig
```

the alternative is to assert only the headers
```typescript
return {
   ...request,
   headers: yourHeaders as AxiosRequestHeaders
}
```

body:
> ```js
> headers: {
>   "Content-type": "application/json",
>   "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": "*"
> }
> ```

You don't need either of those headers and `access-control-allow-origin` should **never** be in a request. In fact, it's probably causing the preflight to fail leading to your problem

body:
That is a default config preset, not an interceptor logic.
I see no reason to change the current behavior that has developed historically. If the server does not support this header, there should be no problem. If the server processes the header, then the current value works for most users because it was previously obtained from users troubleshooting.
The header can be easily disabled via default config/instance config or request config, so it seems not a big deal.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Hi, thanks for you answer.
I don't know how historically it was developed but version `0.27.2` (latest version before major) didn't have this header, I know it because I see the differences in my tests
Maybe you're right, maybe having a default `Accept` header with `application/json` is convenient for the 90% (who do RESTful JSON), the question is how a HTTP server that return multiple type of responses will behave when they'll receive `application/json, text/plain, */*` (aka "every format").
Since `Accept` is overridable this issue isn't a bug but [you should inform in the documentation](https://axios-http.com/docs/req_config) the default headers set by `axios`
author: eau-de-la-seine

body:
> but version 0.27.2 (latest version before major) didn't have this header, I know it because I see the differences in my tests

Actually, Axios 0.27.2 has this header, just like the oldest v0.1.0 version. Your issue probably relates to something else.
https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/bc733fec78326609e751187c9d453cee9bf1993a/lib/defaults/index.js#L131-L135
https://codesandbox.io/s/clever-resonance-hbvjtg?file=/src/index.js

> the question is how a HTTP server that return multiple type of responses will behave

I guess if users haven't reported related issues in 10 years, then the current value works for most, even though no quality values are set in the header for explicit prioritization.

> but you should inform in the documentation

Definitely, but to be honest, this is not the biggest lack of documentation, at the same time, this is the initial behavior, which is of little interest to most users.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Hi @DigitalBrainJS , this extract of code may be in the `0.27.2` source code, but I was not seeing it in my request interceptor, maybe it was added **after** the request interceptor.
Anyway now I use the latest `1.3.4` version, I don't encounter the bizarre `common, delete, get, head, post, put, patch` headers from `0.27.2`, and I know that `axios` adds a default `Accept: application/json, text/plain, */*` header if we don't set one, I consider this issue as closed. Thanks

body:
>const axios = require('axios)
>const axiosClient = axios.create(axiosConfig) // broken in browser but work for node app
>const axiosClient = axios.default.create(axiosConfig) // work in browser

in this above example if you use `require` with axios then it gives you a default import.
```js
const axios = require('axios').default
//... code
```

or else u can use module based import like this , it will automatically use default import.
```js
import axios from 'axios';
//... code
```

body:
Just understood the defect

in the .npmrc the correct spelling is noproxy and not no-proxy
It creates the environment variable npm_config_noproxy and not the env var npm_config_no_proxy

IN axios you;re looking at the bad environment variable.
For example if I odify my .npmrc and rename noproxy in no-proxy axios works correctly, but all my other npm services are no more working.

body:
> Hi, I also encountered this issue on axios 1.6.1. Setting noproxy in .npmrc doesn't work. Setting environment variable NO_PROXY is a temporary workaround. May I ask what is the current status of this issue?

Oops, this fell off my radar. I've set a reminder for myself to look at publishing a `proxy-from-env` update this weekend.
author: Rob--W

body:
The issue here is that proxy-from-env looks at npm_config_http_proxy and npm_config_https_proxy
WHY? That's ridiculous. We're not running npm, we're running our software. The npm config proxies are for npm to get its modules. Axios shouldn't be using npm configs for doing non-npm things. 
I doubt proxy-from-env is going to change (there's zero activity there for over a year) but I think Axios should seriously consider not using it. Go back to manually checking the http_proxy, https_proxy and no_proxy environment variables. That's it.
noproxy is NOT the solution here - I can't list EVERY host I use in the noproxy just because I want npm to talk to nexus using a proxy.
The contents of ~/.npmrc should have ZERO effect on any software other than npm. Certainly not MY software that uses Axios. This took me an entire day to debug and now I have to break npm to make my software work.
author: KrayzeeKev

body:
> The issue here is that proxy-from-env looks at npm_config_http_proxy and npm_config_https_proxy
> WHY?

Because people wanted a way to rely on npm config, in https://github.com/Rob--W/proxy-from-env/issues/8

In retrospect that should have been opt-in.

> The contents of ~/.npmrc should have ZERO effect on any software other than npm. Certainly not MY software that uses Axios.

I agree. I'm going to work on a 2.0.0 release that doesn't look at npm vars by default, but that exports a method called `getProxyFromEnvAndNpmConfig` for those who really want to read from the npm config for some reason. I'm going to track that work at https://github.com/Rob--W/proxy-from-env/issues/13
author: Rob--W

body: Awesome. Perhaps maybe something even more generic where you can provide an arbitrary prefix or prefixes for the variables so you can call it with "npm_config_" but you could also call it with "curl_" or whatever else.
author: KrayzeeKev

> 
> WHY? That's ridiculous. We're not running npm, we're running our software. The npm config proxies are for npm to get its modules. Axios shouldn't be using npm configs for doing non-npm things.

> I doubt proxy-from-env is going to change (there's zero activity there for over a year) but I think Axios should seriously consider not using it. Go back to manually checking the http_proxy, https_proxy and no_proxy environment variables. That's it.
> 
> noproxy is NOT the solution here - I can't list EVERY host I use in the noproxy just because I want npm to talk to nexus using a proxy.
> 
> The contents of ~/.npmrc should have ZERO effect on any software other than npm. Certainly not MY software that uses Axios. This took me an entire day to debug and now I have to break npm to make my software work.

agreed. this baffled me for months until out of fortune someone told me about this. i think it is reasonable to assume process launched from npm scripts to respect proxy environment variables like `http_proxy` for `python` or `curl`. But it's really a surprise to learn .npmrc proxy settings affect a nodejs process.

I was able to solve the problem by modifying the 'define' section in vite.config.ts. 
If I remove this section, or change it to` define: { global: "globalThis" }` the build works fine and the deployment is successful without any issues. 
It is suspected that there was a problem with handling axios as a global object during the vite build process.

author: alerousseau

body:
> I am trying to open around 5600 URLs concurrently with a [throat](https://www.npmjs.com/package/throat) limiter limiting maximum concurrent requests to 5 at a time.

@eXhumer Is there a possibility that you are trying to save references to all 5600 response objects inside Promise.all? 
Unlikely that Axios can be the leak reason, as it has no global references or listeners, it all depends on how the dev using it.

> Replacing axios to instead use fetch fixes the issue for me. There is no longer any more memory leak and the memory usage with fetch never exceeds 300MB.

fetch simply uses less memory, so even if you store 5600 responses, the total memory occupied might be within the limits.

P.S If redirects are not used, then in node.js maxRedirects should be set to 0, since follow-redirects use a lot of memory to buffer the request.
author: DigitalBrainJS
eXhumer

body:
@eXhumer This seems to be exactly what you are doing.
You have references to all the promises via array, and every promise keeps a reference to its value (response) after it becomes resolved. That is why mem consumption grows after each promise is resolved. GC cannot release occupied memory due to the presence of an alive reference to a resolved promise. You should at least remove the resolved & handled promises from the array.

You should at least remove the resolved & handled promises from the array.

author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Based on the small research, this seems a genuine network error reported by the browser (aka wifi had a blip, or docker containers do their interrupt-all-network-connections-on-the-host dance). See https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/b3be36585884ba1e237fdd0eacf55f678aefc396/lib/adapters/xhr.js#L162 .

So, this should happen under normal circumstances in the real world. Retry it, or ignore, or such..
author: robinp

body:
We had the same issue that `"AxiosError: Network Error"` occasionally happens less than 1% of entire requests with our app based on Vue3, and the problem seems solved by adding retrying requests.

Same as @robinp mentioned, we got errors here.

https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/b3be36585884ba1e237fdd0eacf55f678aefc396/lib/adapters/xhr.js#L158-L162

The situation for us was we removed Nuxt.js from our app and migrated to a pure Vue3 app recently, then the errors came to happen occasionally since we released our Vue3 app.
Although Nuxt has `nuxt/axios` that bundles `axios-retry`, We forgot to install `axios-retry` to our Vue3 app and the error sometimes became to happen. Thus, we installed it, and the errors have stopped so far. Thanks.

Here we are with the same problem, happening in a very small percentage of requests, in our react native mobile application. In our case, we have always used axios-retry. Some affected users report that it only happens on a specific connection. However, we were unable to advance the investigations.
Anyone with a similar situation that managed to resolve it?

author: abhi9dev

body:
We're suddenly having this issue, roughly 10% of the daily traffic in Vue 2 application since 10-April 2025, mostly on mobile browsers. The frontend flow is that we call an endpoint at the router hook level and also cache the response in memory. On Sentry report we don't see the `response` entry, just the `request` alone and it's tricky to replicate or find the root cause.

The hypothesis is that there might be an interruption in the way the user interacts with our website and it cancels the API calls e.g.:

- The user might refresh the tab right after they navigate to the new page and the API call is not completed yet.
- The user might experience slow network and swipes between pages in the middle of API calls.

But still, not sure if it's the correct answer to this issue.

author: ssompally

body:
i highly doubt this has to do with axios actually, this error can happen pretty routinely depending on the users internet stability.

in axios itself you can easily write up an adapter to handle this and try reconnecting or do any number of other things, there is even a package https://www.npmjs.com/package/axios-retry that provides this adaptor

as for the sentry logs, once again you can very easily filter out this and other errors like abort etc as these should probably not be logged to sentry anyways as this is normally not seen as a error that requires intervention as there is pretty much nothing one can do to debug and replication is quite hard.

i will mark this as both something to add to the docs and maybe include some helpful recipes.

body:
Looks like it is the same as above code. Please read the [Module Mocking in ESM section](https://jestjs.io/docs/ecmascript-modules#module-mocking-in-esm) carefully.

The point is that in ESM (or with `transform: {}`) there is no hoisting of `jest.mock()` calls. In other words, calling `jest.mock('axios')` after `import axios from 'axios';` has no effect on the imported module, because the module was already evaluated. As you know in ESM all `import` statements are evaluated first, so there would be no effect even if you would reloader lines of your code.

The solution was in the provided link, which you did ignore for some reason.

body: Yes, the `false` value is taken into account by the `set` method and its `rewrite` option in the AxiosHeader API. Therefore, such a value will not be overwritten unless the `rewrite` option is explicitly specified in the method. Axios logic relies on this to allow the user to opt out of installing unwanted and/or default headers.

body: The issue you're encountering may be related to a known behavior in Axios, where the timeout configuration option only applies to the time it takes for the server to start sending a response, not the time it takes to finish sending the entire response. If the server starts sending the response but takes too long to finish, Axios will not timeout as expected.

body: If you've confirmed that the GCP Internal Load Balancer responded within 5 seconds, but the response still isn't reaching the client end, there might be few factors causing the delay. Some possible reasons include network delays between your Cloud Run service and the GCP Internal Load Balancer, or resource constraints in your Cloud Run service. Monitor network latency between your Cloud Run service and the GCP Internal Load Balancer. Also check the resource usage and performance of your Cloud Run service.

body:
I am a beginner to open source and all this is pretty new to me but I will try my best.
Upon searching the web I think your `axios.delete()` syntax is indeed correct and it should work fine.

You can take a look at this stack overflow website where I found out that it is possible to attach body to delete request (although
not recommended). 
[Axios - DELETE Request With Request Body and Headers?](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51069552/axios-delete-request-with-request-body-and-headers)
It clearly states:
```javascript
axios.delete('url', { data: payload }).then(
  // Observe the data keyword this time. Very important
  // payload is the request body
  // Do something
)
```
It also says:
 - `delete` requests with a body need it to be set under a `data` key

Also I found out this issue on axios which dicusses about having a body in a delete request:
 - #897 
 - https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/897#issuecomment-343715381

In the above comment it is mentioned that:

`axiox.delete` does support a request body. It accepts two parameters: `url` and optional `config`. You can use `config.data` to set the response body as follows:
```javascript
axios.delete(url, { data: { foo: "bar" } });
```
`post`, `put`, and `patch` accept 3 parameters: `url`, `data`, and `config` so you can you can use the second parameter to set the response body like this:
```javascript
axios.put(url, { foo: "bar" });
```
Hope it helps!

body:
Axios is an isomorphic library. This error indicates that your build tool is not configured properly to build the client-related code, instead, it tries to build the Node.js version of Axios and then use it on the client, polyfilling the node.js dependencies. Axios for browsers does not have any dependencies.
So to fix this, you need to figure out how to configure your bundler to resolve the client-side code of the dependencies.

body:
The problem comes with the `./debug` in `follow-redirects` package.
I managed to remove the alert like this:
Install the `debug` package.

`npm install debug`

By doing this you remove this alert but a new appears:

`Module not found: Can't resolve 'supports-color' in './node_modules/debug/src'`

There is an issue with `supports-color` [*github issue here*](https://github.com/debug-js/debug/issues/912) in versions +9 so you will have to install `"supports-color": "^8.1.1"`

`npm install supports-color@8.1.1`

This worked for me, hope its works for you two : )

body:
I use yarn berry and I got a similar warning. I fixed it by adding the following config to `.yarnrc.yml`. Once you're done, remember to run `yarn` to apply the change. 

```
packageExtensions:
  axios@*:
    dependencies:
      debug: "*"
  debug@*:
    dependencies:
      supports-color: "*"
```

body: A minor correction: above I say the host header and the port header are set correctly to the proxy. But these are not http request headers, these are internal properties of the Axios/node.js request instance used for addressing the http Request to a server. Simply said: the http request is sent correctly to the hostname/IP address and port of the proxy and not directly to the http server.

body:
This `encode` function is a custom encoding function that does the same thing as `encodeURIComponent`, but with a few exceptions. `encodeURIComponent` is a built-in JavaScript function that encodes special characters. In general, encoding these characters is necessary because they can be misinterpreted by the browser or server when included in a URL.

For example, the colon `:` is used to separate the protocol (like http or https) from the rest of the URL, so if you had a colon in the path part of your URL, it could cause problems. Similarly, the dollar sign `$` and the comma `,` can have special meanings in different contexts and should be encoded to avoid misinterpretation. Plus sign `+` is often treated as a space character in URLs, and brackets `[` and `]` are used in URLs to denote IPv6 addresses.

So, the `encodeURIComponent` function is used to encode these special characters to avoid any issues. However, this function also encodes some characters that you might not want to encode. This is where the `replace` calls come into play. They replace the encoded versions of `:`, `$`, `,`, `+`, `[`, and `]` with the actual characters, effectively "un-encoding" them.

In summary, this function encodes all special characters, but then "un-encodes" certain ones. This could be useful in situations where you want most special characters to be encoded, but a few to be left as-is. The specific characters to be left as-is would depend on the requirements of the specific use case. In this example, it's because the code block which follows that uses the encoded values to produce a URI which would likely error in an application context if the data being passed in contains certain special characters but not necessarily all. 

Hope that helps! Feel free to close this if so or comment back if you have more specific questions! 👍

Similarly, the dollar sign $ and the comma , can have special meanings in different contexts and should be encoded to avoid misinterpretation. Plus sign + is often treated as a space character in URLs, and brackets [ and ] are used in URLs to denote IPv6 addresses.

@drod1107 
Thank you very much for your reply, but I still don't understand why these characters need to be replaced back.
If everyone follows the standard encode and decode rules, there shouldn't be any problems.

Can you give a specific example where this replacement is necessary?
author: uinz

body:
Ideally, in good development I should think it won't come up much, if at all. But... 

Let's say your API services a database for a company and the purpose of the DB is tracking employee training. Some employees take a course called "Service+" in the DB, or maybe "C#".

You could, of course, alias these. You could do a lot of things, and most of them are likely a better practice than the replacement method, IMO. 

But this replacement method is one way to do the job while ensuring that the DB reflects the data passed in EXACTLY to the character, without confusing the API with characters that would otherwise short circuit the URI path, and vice versa - the DB can offer an option to select from that includes a special character and the URI isn't shortcircuited by that character, and the user still sees the character on their end. 

Does that make more sense? Or did I misunderstand your question?

body:
Header keys are [case insensitive anyway](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7230#section-3.2).

> Each header field consists of a **case-insensitive** field name followed
   by a colon (":"), optional leading whitespace, the field value, and
   optional trailing whitespace.

Why would this matter? If Apache Superset Service handles this incorrectly, then it is a bug that should be raised with the maintainers

body:
I found the issue. Posting the answer here just in case others are facing the same problem.

`Axios.get` apparently only takes 2 arguments (`url` and `config`). Since `Axios.post` takes in 3 arguments (`url`, `data` and `config`) - I was under the impression GET would be the same but I was mistaken.

So the correct version of the above code is:

```js
// handling the axios request
getZipFile: ({}) => Axios.get('/zipfile, {
  params: {...},
  responseType: 'arraybuffer'
})
```

Basically, `params` and `responseType` is at the same level within the config. The other configs like `responseEncoding` etc were not needed.

body: The same, It seems that the issue is caused by the default `transformRequest` behavior. I guess that you have passed a formatted value using the method "JSON.stringify". eg: In my project, I encountered this issue when upgrading the version from **v0.21.1** to **v0.21.2**. After investigation, I discovered that the problem was caused by the following changes， and It transformed the data into the format like: `JSON.stringify(JSON.stringify(xxxxx))` In version **v0.27.2**，The inferred condition is that： ``` var contentType = headers && headers['Content-Type']; if (isObjectPayload || contentType === 'application/json') ``` And in version **v1.0.0**, it's: ``` const contentType = headers.getContentType() || ''; const hasJSONContentType = contentType.indexOf('application/json') > -1; if (isObjectPayload || hasJSONContentType ) ```

This was quite confusing when I stumbled upon it.
Yes, sure, it's not technically good practice to send the `content-type` header without a request body, but sometimes you rely on APIs that are not well made. When those situations occur, you don't want your http library to block your path forward.

This line of code is too opinionated and can actually completely block users from accomplishing what they want to do with `axios`, please remove:

body:
end of investigation

I end up on this article : https://expeditedsecurity.com/blog/nginx-brotli/
saying : 

> the brotli command is turned up to max by default. (...) Brotli with setting 4 is both significantly smaller AND compresses faster than gzip

Indeed, launching nestjs with the following code results in fast and compressed http response.

```
import { constants } from 'zlib';
...
await app.register<FastifyCompressOptions>(compression, { brotliOptions: { params: { [constants.BROTLI_PARAM_QUALITY]: 4 } } });
```

So, definitely not a problem with axios. Just a setup problem with either server application setup or node zlib brotli default compression level.

I will open an issue for the nestjs documentation
author: superkeil

body:
This is [a known Chrome XMLHttpRequest issue](https://stackoverflow.com/a/47287125/11046238) with compressed responses. The hack to manually get the Content-Length header won't work because in Chrome the downloaded bytes refer to uncompressed size, while you only have the total compressed size.
P.S. There is a problem with variable shading in your code.

body: This is not actually a new behavior, but it results from a bug fix with the merging of `Content-Type` in common headers. The feature it applies to was released with the major semver tag, but the `Content-Type` set by the common headers was simply ignored and the bug went unnoticed for a long time until #5825 was reported. Now some users will consider this a bug fix, others - breaking change. Thus it's quite a difficult question how to do it in a way that is acceptable to both parties. Fixing this issue essentially means reverting the fix for bug #5825.

> On the documentation page for Multipart Bodies (https://axios-http.com/docs/multipart) there is a line that reads:

body:
I was facing the same issue.

The cookie sent by the server was getting save with server domain(You can verify this hitting any of your server URL from browser search bar and openning dev tools) as a result when the api call was happening from browser in client domain cookie was not getting sent. Everything was working fine in localhost.

The way I solved this was to put both server(render app) and client app(vercel) behind a reverse proxy server(render). Now since for browser the domain is same(proxy server domain) It started sending cookie.

author: gesirdek

body:
Generally, if the cookies are being sent (as in, in the response, you have the set-cookie flag, which includes the correct cookies you want, say sessionid, csrftoken cookie), but the browser isn't storing them, it's usually an issue with additional flags that the browser needs. 

It needs the `access-control-allow-origin` to point to your frontend, `access-control-allow-credentials` to be true, so the browser knows to allow subsequent requests, and is allowed to send cookies. It looks like you've done this, but just make sure in dev tools. 

You also need to make sure the cookies you send (if cross origin, which in your case it is, since front and back are on different domains):

- are set to Secure (so it's only sent over HTTPS), 
- and SameSite is set to None (if you don't set SameSite manually to None, it defaults to Lax, and browsers reject cross origin cookies where SameSite is Lax. 
- path points to the correct path (it defaults to '/' which is usually correct, but if you're trying to set different cookies per different paths, then look into this)

More info about this [here](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Set-Cookie#samesitesamesite-value). 

So your set-cookie header should look something like this: 

sessionid=z2s5v7c5wwrt1kpc7bs12n7qclmgbcav; expires=Sun, 14 Jan 2024 19:25:28 GMT; **`HttpOnly`**; Max-Age=1209600; Path=/; **`SameSite=None`**; **`Secure`**

It works locally because it's treated as same origin cookies, which are considered safe (since the origin and host are the same).

body:
Hi there,
I'm relatively new to this project, so please correct me if I'm mistaken.
i have read your commits (file )and come with idea that:-
Axios may not forcefully terminate the request while it's still expecting more data to come.So,i think in that case timeout function will not work for you rather We have to make a separate timeout function in addition to axios's timeout function.By implementing this custom timeout mechanism, you should have better control over requests with chunked responses and ensure that the timeout is respected
author: SagarKapoorin

body:
Yes exactly, once the first chunk is received, Axios drops the timeout and considers the request answered in time. This I think raises the debate on at what moment we consider the request timed out - at the first byte received or the last byte received. 

Initially yes, I was also thinking of the custom timeout function and wrapping the axios request into `Promise.race([])` with that timeout function.
But I found a workaround for this issue (the issue in my case, as it generally looks like designed this way), the solution is to use AbortSignal. It cancels the request regardless, see the example:

```javascript
	const AXIOS_TIMEOUT = 5000;

   axios.request({
        method: "POST",
        url,
        timeout: AXIOS_TIMEOUT,
		signal: AbortSignal.timeout(AXIOS_TIMEOUT), // Ensures the request will be cancelled
    })
```

I wrote a detailed text about the technique in [the article](https://nothing-else.blog/blog/axios-timeout-for-chunked-responses)

I would still love to clarify this behavior in the documentation

body:
> @scott-knight The problem is that this code should not be included and executed on the frontend side at all, it's server-side only.
> 

@DigitalBrainJS - Axios is a JS library, correct? Using it to make API calls from the client side, which is the only use-case I've seen it used for, is what it does well (when it compiles correctly). As far as `bundler configuration`, there's literally nothing to configure. We don't have any settings for the bundler configuration. We use ESBuild, which is quite literally the simplest bundling setup that can be used to to manage JS libraries within a Rails app; there's nothing to it. We used to add the axios library via Yarn, then import Axios as a module in within a stimulus controller. Once imported, we would use axios to make fetch calls to our API or controller endpoints to update portions of our views -- very simple, nothing to it. I'm currently using Node 20.17.0 with ESbuild and Axios still doesn't compile correctly. The Axios library is imported and complied AS-IS. The Axios http library assumes too much of the compiling engine (based on what you've said, the constants should exist -- but what if they dont?). As mentioned, v1.2.0 and older versions compile just fine. Version 1.2.1 or newer breaks when compiled based on the changes in the Axios code. It's fine if you don't want to fix the Axios code. We've moved on, we've removed Axios from our project. It sucks that we've had to do that. We like Axios! It's a great tool. But it's unusable after v1.2.0. 

At some point, I'm going to create clean Rails app to test if Axios compiles properly with ESBuild. If it wont compile in a clean (brand-spanking-new) app, I will open another ticket. Until then, best wishes.
author: scott-knight

body:
@scott-knight 

> Using it to make API calls from the client side, which is the only use-case I've seen it used fo

node.js?

> We don't have any settings for the bundler configuration.

With default settings, it successfully builds Axios from the command line without including the http adaptor (where "undefined" constants are used), just as it should be.
```
esbuild index.js --bundle --outfile=bundle.js
```

bundle.js
```js
  // node_modules/axios/lib/helpers/null.js
  var null_default = null;

  // node_modules/axios/lib/adapters/adapters.js
  var knownAdapters = {
    http: null_default, // <<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<<< http adapter is not included in the build
    xhr: xhr_default,
    fetch: fetch_default
  };
```
> The Axios http library assumes too much of the compiling engine 

Yes, Axios relies on it. Do you know any other approaches to creating an isomorphic cross-platform library?

> It's fine if you don't want to fix the Axios code. 

I'm wondering how you suggest to "fix" this?

> The Axios library is imported and complied AS-IS. 

Here is a sandbox with a minimal example just for you. As we can see- everything works fine.
https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/8f938c

> But it's unusable after v1.2.0.

This means that you always had an incorrectly configured bundle assembly, and a piece of useless Axios server-side code always got to the client build, but since v1.2.0, this code started throwing an exception because of adding references to the constants of the built-in node module, which wasn't mocked by shims. This way you always had unnecessary junk in your build.
The main difference between the server and client Axios builds is that the client build does not include the `http` adapter, where node-specific API is used.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
@DigitalBrainJS none of what you replied to me with is relevant. I showed you where in the code the issue occurs in the axios code, it’s literally at the top of this thread. The fix was posted in this PR https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5976/files. You’ve chosen to ignore it. 

You’ve stated I have bad configurations (bundle assembly) in my app, I have NONE. As mentioned, we use yarn to add the library, and used the js import statement to import Axios. When ESbuild compiles the code for deploy, it finds the flaws in the Axios code (as outlined above at the top of this bug report). It’s not that difficult to comprehend. The only change we made was upgrading Axios from 1.2.0 to 1.2.1, and Axios broke, it doesn’t compile correctly, with the error outlined above. There’s no configuration to make it compile one way or another. 

Additionally, a second person posted in this thread with the same issue, and you’re blaming them for their config too. At what point do you take ownership of your bad code? 

Again, here is the PR for the fix https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5976/files. 

Merge it, don’t merge it. We no longer use Axios, and the issue is closed.
author: scott-knight

body:
@DigitalBrainJS - I want to apologize for not replying with a better described response. I've taken another look at this issue to see if I can explain what I'm seeing and what I think the issue is. I don't want this issue to be dismissed as irrelevant, I don't think it is. Looking at this file: https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/lib/adapters/http.js on line 12, it does an import: `import zlib from 'zlib';`, then [on line 29](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/1472163d373dda71f74e86365ee2f298d922f5db/lib/adapters/http.js#L29C1-L32C3), the Axios code assumes that `zlib.constants.Z_SYNC_FLUSH` will be defined. However, in my case, when ESBuild compiles, it's not. For whatever reason, when ESBuild compiles the Axios code, it's including the contents of `/lib/adapters/http.js`. That being the case, we see this failure when ESBuild looks through the code and is unable to resolve `zlib.constants.Z_SYNC_FLUSH`. Additionally, after doing a quick review of [PR](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/5976/files), it isn't going to fix this bug either, as it doesn't address `zlib.constants.Z_SYNC_FLUSH`. My apologies for insisting it would, the PR should be closed (or updated). Regardless, ESBuild is still checking `/lib/adapters/http.js` when it compiles and is finding this error. My only point was that the `/lib/adapters/http.js` contained this issue when ESBuild tries to compile it. The fix would be simple enough: `zlib?.constants?.Z_SYNC_FLUSH || 2` (or whatever the default value is supposed to be). I assume that safe navigation with dot notation will remedy this issue, but I haven't tested it to make sure. I would ask that you reconsider fixing it. However, I also understand why you wouldn't (based on what you described). If it gets fixed, we may reconsider using Axios at some point (I check it every once in a while). Until then, best wishes.
SIDE NOTE: I ran the [sandbox](https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/8f938c) and it looks like it is unable to import Axios when it tries to start index.js: ![Screenshot 2024-09-17 204905](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0fa4d9e9-00a5-4bc9-aa70-53b139c9b11f)
author: scott-knight

body:
@scott-knight 

> However, in my case, when ESBuild compiles, it's not.

The problem is that your ESBuild for some reason imports an http adapter module into the build. It doesn't matter what code is in this module since it shouldn't be included in the browser bundle and, accordingly, it won't cause errors, since it won't physically be in the bundle.

> Regardless, ESBuild is still checking /lib/adapters/http.js

Yes, and to fix the issue (not to hide) you need to determine why this is happening. 
The latest ESBuild v0.23.1 builds without problems, so check the version of the installed package. 
You can test it on an empty project.

> I ran the [sandbox](https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/8f938c) and it looks like it is unable to import Axios when it tries to start index.js:

Are you sure you are not trying to run the index.js with node? 
You don't need to run anything with node there, just check out the front-end preview that imports a bundle with a simple Axios call built by ESBuild.
[Frontend preview](https://89lfqg-8000.csb.app/)
[Sandbox](https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/axios-esbuild-89lfqg)
author: DigitalBrainJS

body: @DigitalBrainJS - we're running ESBuild v0.23.1. We dont have any settings that would force ESBuild to parse all files within the Axios code (or any other code for that matter). The issue is that it does. Looking through the ESBuild documentation, I havent found anything that would allow or disallow file-level discrimination. ESBuild does what it's going to do. My request would be to harden the Axios code against such an issue (using the fix I posted previously). I'm not sure what I can say to convince you to do this fix. As it seems the fix will not be implemented, I'm going to move on. Best wishes.

As I said ESBuild builds Axios normally with default configuration from their site, I provided a working example to confirm this, meanwhile, I haven't seen a reproducible example that confirms at least any problem (either with Axios or ESbuild). I don't know what else can be added here.

author: scott-knight

body:
>  but have to include commonjs modules to support other libraries.

@nsallis Axios is also built using rollup. All you need to do is tell the `plugin-node-resolve` plugin to use the `browser` target platform by setting `resolve({browser: true})` option. This is required for any browser build with rollup, regardless of whether you use Axios or not. 

From their docs:
> browser
> Type: Boolean
> Default: false
> 
> If true, instructs the plugin to use the browser module resolutions in package.json and adds 'browser' to exportConditions if it is not present so browser conditionals in exports are applied. If false, any browser properties in package files will be ignored. Alternatively, a value of 'browser' can be added to both the mainFields and exportConditions options, however this option takes precedence over mainFields.

https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/bad6d8b97b52c0c15311c92dd596fc0bff122651/rollup.config.js#L37-L38

body:
hey all! 
We've just experienced this issue over at Stencil - https://github.com/stenciljs/core/issues/6202 (we just migrated from Rollup 2 - 4 so are experiencing a number of teething issues).

Stencil wraps Rollup so obvs difficult to produce a suitable repro however, the relevant rollup config is [here](https://github.com/stenciljs/core/blob/main/src/compiler/bundle/bundle-output.ts#L60-L66) - the fix for us seems to be to get `nodeResolve` (via export conditions) to also match with `exports` > `require` fields:

```ts
rollupNodeResolvePlugin({
    ...
    browser: true,
    exportConditions: ['default', 'module', 'import', 'require'], // < default is ['default', 'module', 'import']
    ...
  });
```
My feeling is that axios' combination of `browser`> `require` is probably unusual?

Perhaps `browser` should also have an `import` / `module` which points to `./dist/esm/axios.js` (or maybe even the `default` should, instead of `./index.js`)

That's how Blobs work, so it's not Axios' issue. 
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/16846382/memory-usage-with-xhr-blob-responsetype-chrome?rq=3
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61567890/how-to-fix-browser-blob-download-from-maxing-ram
Why can't you use direct download from a link? There is little point in using a client library to download a file if the browser can download the file from a link in the background.
Until the [File system API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/File_System_API#browser_compatibility) is available for most browsers and Axios gets streaming support, this is the only way to download huge files using the fetch/xhr client.

body:
I described GET requests can exceed the timeout. On the other hand, I found POST requests with a big payload throws a timeout error when the request spends over the timeout.  This behavior depends on follow-redirects which axios uses internally and it is different from the standard `socket.setTimeout()`.  This difference between GET and POST is unexpected one and I found some issues on the follow-redirects repository.
- https://github.com/follow-redirects/follow-redirects/issues/181
- https://github.com/follow-redirects/follow-redirects/issues/186

I think this issue is not only document problem. It's better if you could fix axios (or follow-redirects).

author: ipatalas

body:
Axios doesn't handle timeouts itself but just passes the timeout value to the low-level request library. The problem is that `follow-redirects` is too outdated and doesn't support the `destroy` method to abort the request, like the native node request. (in practice it destroys the stream but not the underlying request and timers). As we know `abort` method was deprecated a long time ago, so Axios 1.x was moved to the current API spec and this is the reason for the issue. 
I have created PR for `follow-redirects` but it can take some time to be reviewed & merged, plus it seems there are some issues with their CI. But I hope everything will be resolved soon.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body: @DigitalBrainJS Thanks for the PR, I'll get on it (will need some tests etc.). Could you just help me understand the connection with this issue? The PR is described as a fix for a resource leak, but the issue I see here is a delayed reaction. Understanding the link will help me write the right tests.
author: RubenVerborgh

body:
@RubenVerborgh It is a temporary resource leak. Resources will be released on timeout, but not when we call `destroy` method.
In the current node http request API specification, aborting a request must be done using the `destroy` method (+`close` event), since `abort` and `abort` event have been deprecated since version 14 (https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#requestabort).
Follow-redirects does not have a `destroy` implementation, so it cannot release internal resources when the user calls the `destroy` method, as required by the specification and it just destroys the writable stream. 
Axios 1.x uses the destroy method to abort the request, so we have a temporary resource leak (timer and internal HTTP request) that keeps the node running after the user aborts the request. This works with native HTTP request, but not with follow-redirects implementation.

After the fix or with native HTTP request:
```
[19:22:29.518] INFO (15572): Started on v16.19.0 using Axios 1.5.0
[19:22:30.528] INFO (15572): Error: CanceledError: canceled
[19:22:30.529] INFO (15572): Request was aborted 1.3651968
[19:22:30.530] INFO (15572): Done 1.3664275
[19:22:30.549] INFO (15572): Child process exited with 0  

Process finished with exit code 0

```

without the fix:

```
[19:24:50.051] INFO (6740): Started on v16.19.0 using axios 1.5.0
[19:24:51.073] INFO (6740): Error: CanceledError: canceled
[19:24:51.074] INFO (6740): Request was aborted 1.6211567
[19:24:51.075] INFO (6740): Done 1.6224202

There are 11 handle(s) keeping the process running

# TTYWRAP
(unknown stack trace)

# Timeout
(unknown stack trace)

# TCPWRAP
(unknown stack trace)

# TLSWRAP
(unknown stack trace)

# DNSCHANNEL
(unknown stack trace)

# HTTPCLIENTREQUEST
(unknown stack trace)

# Timeout                                                                                                           
H:\JS\forks\axios\trash\cancel\node_modules\follow-redirects\index.js:168 - self._timeout = setTimeout(function () {
                                                                                                                    
# TickObject                                                                                                        
(unknown stack trace)                                                                                               
                                                                                                                    
# TickObject                                                                                                        
(unknown stack trace)                                                                                               

# TickObject
(unknown stack trace)

# TickObject
(unknown stack trace)

[19:24:55.756] INFO (6740): Child process exited with 0

Process finished with exit code 0

```
author: DigitalBrainJS

body: Thanks! Updated fix published as `follow-redirects@1.15.3`.

author: AhmedMustafa505

body:
Axios doesn't do anything special with the FormData payload on the client side and doesn't have any platform-specific code for ReactNative. FormData is passed directly to the XMLHttpRequest, so this is an RN platform issue. They seem to have their own implementation of FormData with some spec incompatibility that prevents native blobs from being used as payload.
[Judging by the comment](https://github.com/facebook/react-native/blob/030663bb0634fc76f811cdc63e4d09e7ca32f3d4/packages/react-native/Libraries/Network/FormData.js#L78C1-L82C48) in the code and the non-standard interface, they basically [do not support appending Blobs](https://github.com/facebook/react-native/issues/35698#issuecomment-1441579017)

> In fact, using the FormData object in React Native is not similar to the web. You should pass a FormDataValue as second paramater of the append function : FormDataValue = **string | {name?: string, type?: string, uri: string}**

So this is definitely the target platform issue. There is no solution here, except perhaps port the functionality with our own FormData encoder that Axios uses on the server side, but given the current architecture without plugins functionality, this would seriously increase the size of the client bundle, which is unacceptable for the browser.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Hello Ayoub-Chebbi, hope you're doing well.
If you are not getting any response or errors when clicking the button to add a car part, there could be several reasons for this issue. Here are some steps to help you troubleshoot and resolve the problem:

1. Check Your Backend API:
Ensure that your backend API is running and listening on http://localhost:8000. You can try accessing the API endpoint directly in your browser or using tools like Postman to verify that it's functioning correctly.
Error Handling:

2. Network Request:
Open your browser's developer tools and go to the "Network" tab. Try submitting the form again and check if there is a network request being made to your API. If there's no network request, it indicates that the form submission isn't triggering the Axios request.

3. Form Submission:
Make sure that your form submission button (<input type="submit" value="Submit" />) is working as expected and is triggering the handleSubmit function. You can add a console.log statement inside the handleSubmit function to check if it's being called when you click the submit button.

4. Form Fields:
Ensure that all the form fields have valid values before submitting the form. You can add some basic validation to check if required fields are not empty.

5. CORS Issue:
If your frontend is running on a different port or domain than your backend, you might encounter Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) issues. Make sure your backend is configured to allow requests from your frontend's domain. You can use CORS middleware for this functionality.


By following these steps and debugging each part of your code, you should be able to identify and resolve the issue with your form submission not working. If you encounter specific error messages or issues, feel free to provide more details, and I can assist further.

> I see that axios v1.6.0 is now tested for Node.js 20 - so this issue can be closed:
> 
> * [fix(dns): fixed lookup function decorator to work properly in node 20.x; #6011](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/6011)
> * [chore(tests): fixed tests to pass in node v19 and v20 with `keep-alive` enabled; #6021](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/6021)
> 
> There are still problems with axios v1.6.2 and Node.js 20 resulting in ECONNRESET on localhost HTTP requests - my current workaround is this:
> 
> ```ts
>  axios.create({
>     httpAgent: new Agent({ keepAlive: false })
>   });
> ```
> 
> The Node.js issue is still open: [nodejs/node#47130](https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/47130)

author: Pharb

body:
For us it's also happening with remote calls, but I guess the URL does not matter here.

The most important thing is that while the workaround works[^1], it cannot be easily applied to projects, because axios is very often [^2] a dependency of some 3rd party SDKs. Sometimes the packages allow you to pass some axios config to initialize them, but it is not common. And I'm not sure if that's even possible to override the axios config globally for all deps.

So, unfortunately, I think we still need to wait for the resolution of the issue on Node's side (https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/47130)

[^1]: I did not test it myself, but I believe you 😄 
[^2]: I'd even say it's a de-facto standard HTTP library

body: Current v1.x supports `File` bodies in the Node HTTP adapter path. The default transform preserves `File`, and the HTTP adapter handles `utils.isBlob(data) || utils.isFile(data)` by setting content headers and streaming the body. Closing as fixed.

body:
Also seeing this issue, and it appears to be related to this issue #891 as the workaround described in the comments there does work, but it is certainly not ideal as we now need to enforce that developers only use the "long form" for post requests with axios.

I've added all sorts of logging, and even logged through the use of a request interceptor and discovered that the header is already missing by the time it gets logged in the request interceptor, but using a `put` request, or a `get` request, this same issue is not present. 

Since this original issue seems to date back to 2017, its too bad there hasn't been a solution or any insight as to why this issue even exists to begin with.

body:
I had a similar issue sending form data so instead used the axios serializer as described in the [axios multipart docs](https://axios-http.com/docs/multipart).
From:
```
const formData = new FormData();
formData.append('image', upload);
axios.post('http://localhost:3001/upload', formData, {headers: {'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data'}})
```
To:
```
axios.post('http://localhost:3001/upload', { 'image': upload}, {headers: {'Content-Type': 'multipart/form-data'}})
```
Not sure why it fails to send the form data properly (using `axios@1.7.7`)

body:
If you’re still looking for an iOS fix, here’s the approach that worked for me:

**Expo projects**
Add this snippet to app.config.(ts|js):
```
ios: {
        supportsTablet: true,
        userInterfaceStyle: 'automatic',
        bundleIdentifier: 'your.bundle.identifier',
        infoPlist: {
            NSAppTransportSecurity: {
                NSAllowsArbitraryLoads: true,
                NSAllowsLocalNetworking: true,
                NSExceptionDomains: {
                    'yourdomain': {
                        NSExceptionAllowsInsecureHTTPLoads: true,
                        NSIncludesSubdomains: true,
                        NSExceptionRequiresForwardSecrecy: false,
                        NSTemporaryExceptionMinimumTLSVersion: 'TLSv1.1',
                    },
                },
            },
        },
    }
```
**Pure React Native**
Insert the same App Transport Security (ATS) block into Info.plist:
```    
<key>NSAppTransportSecurity</key>
    <dict>
      <key>NSAllowsArbitraryLoads</key>
      <true/>
      <key>NSAllowsLocalNetworking</key>
      <true/>
      <key>NSExceptionDomains</key>
      <dict>
        <key>yourdomain</key>
        <dict>
          <key>NSExceptionAllowsInsecureHTTPLoads</key>
          <true/>
          <key>NSIncludesSubdomains</key>
          <true/>
          <key>NSExceptionRequiresForwardSecrecy</key>
          <false/>
          <key>NSTemporaryExceptionMinimumTLSVersion</key>
          <string>TLSv1.1</string>
        </dict>
      </dict>
    </dict>
```

**Disable SSL pinning in Axios (this should be added after you changed the info.plist file)** 
Using react-native-ssl-pinning, create an “insecure” adapter:

```
import { ReactNativeSSLPinning, fetch as sslFetch } from 'react-native-ssl-pinning'
const insecureAdapter = async (config: AxiosRequestConfig): Promise<AxiosResponse> => {
    const response = await sslFetch(config.url!, {
        method: config.method?.toUpperCase() as ReactNativeSSLPinning.Options['method'],
        sslPinning: { certs: [] },
        disableAllSecurity: true,
        timeoutInterval: config.timeout,
        headers: config.headers as ReactNativeSSLPinning.Options['headers'],
        body: config.data,
    })

    return {
        data: await response.json(),
        status: response.status,
        statusText: '',
        headers: response.headers,
        config: config as InternalAxiosRequestConfig,
    }
}

const axiosInstance = axios.create({ adapter: insecureAdapter })
```

body:
> ### To Reproduce
> 1. Start a new project using the latest version of Next.js by running the following command:  `npx create-next-app@latest`. Then, install the latest version of the Axios library with this command: `npm i axios`
> 2. Create an Axios instance with the following configuration, which enables cross-site request forgery (CSRF) protection by including credentials in requests:
> ```
>   const instance = axios.create({
>     withCredentials: true,
>   });
> ```
> 
> 3. Install the XSRF-TOKEN cookie with specific attributes. Set the cookie value "whatever" and configuring it for the "localhost" domain with strict same-site policy:
> 
> ```
>     const cookies = new Cookies();
>     cookies.set("XSRF-TOKEN", "whatever", {
>       domain: "localhost",
>       sameSite: "strict",
>     });
> ```

Is the Axios CSRF vulnerability a false positive for users who do not have the `withCredentials` property activated?
Under what conditions is the Axios CSRF vulnerability a false positive?
author: syukronarie

Hi guys!
Thank you for your helpful comments.
Let me describe our case.
In a nutshell, we can't just upgrade to the latest version of `axios` right now, as that would break our reporters running in a Node.js 10 environment.
Yes, we know it's an outdated version of the engine, but our stats say it's still being used by our users with ~7k runs per month.
So this update should be made in the next major version of our library, which will drop support for Node.js 10.
At the moment we are sticking to the correlation between client libraries and server versions, so we need to discuss this approach internally to change it or find another solution.

If you have any useful insights that can help us solve this problem without changing the current versioning approach, it would be greatly appreciated.

author: rjewsbfd

body: Assuming `null` and `false` are the same thing in headers and summarily removing them without an option to keep them is pretty gnarly.  It is usually safer to be explicit about what we're doing.  Assuming behavior by way of something being absent is dangerous.  We need to explicitly set a header to `true` or `false`.  We don't want the absence of that header to be the same as `false`.  Amongst other things, that could be a security risk.  Someone doesn't even have to know the header to send in order to indicate `false` for that header on the receiving side.  What about adding an option to keep them with default behavior being as-is today?

author: PromiseJoseph

body: The code change that was made in 1.6.0 was this (from #6028): My reading of this is that it means that the CSRF token will only be sent if the target of the HTTP request is the domain that Axios is running from. I guess that will work fine if it was the Axios domain that generated the CSRF token in the first place but will create a problem for anyone doing this: - SPA on app.mydomain.com - API on api.mydomain.com ...i.e. where the API does the backend auth and issues CSRF tokens which are then used by the SPA. This is a common software architecture. ### Proposal Add a `trustedDomains` setting or something similar to Axios config which the CSRF can be sent to (i.e. when request target domain <> request origin domain). ...unless there is a better fix of course

author: StefanPrintezis

body:
To resolve the issue, you need to set both the **withXSRFToken** and **withCredentials** parameters to **true**.

`const BaseService = axios.create({
    timeout: 60000,
    baseURL: appConfig.apiPrefix,
    withCredentials: true,
    withXSRFToken: true,
})`
author: AlaaKanaan

body:
> Do recent versions have something like "axios.isCancel(thrown)" that was in depreacated "CancelToken"?

Yes, you can still use the isCancel method in version 1.x. This is part of CanceledError functionality, not CancelToken. Additionally, each AxiosError instance has code to determine the type of error. CanceledError has a value of `'ERR_CANCELED'`.

body:
> Same issue here, any workaround ?

In my case, I just changed the code like this:
```js
// axios.post('xxx', [1, 2], { params: { query: JSON.stringify({ filter: true }) } })
axios.post('xxx' + `?query=${encodeURIComponent(JSON.stringify({ filter: true}))}`, [1, 2])
```

body:
The proper method for removing an interceptor is to store its value in a variable and subsequently utilize the eject function.
``` js
const myInterceptor = axios.interceptors.request.use(function () {/*...*/});
axios.interceptors.request.eject(myInterceptor);
```

Alternatively, if you wish to remove all interceptors:

```js
instance.interceptors.request.clear(); // Removes interceptors from requests
```

If you want to update an interceptor, it is advisable to eject the existing interceptor and then add the new one.

See https://github.com/axios/axios#interceptors

body: The cause of this issue is the way `follow-redirects` implements timeout. It has problems when dealing with keep-alive connections. A simple fix is to remove the `timeout` event handler when the request finishes, but I'm not sure this will work for all use-cases, as that library is mostly geared towards earlier node versions.

body:
Has anyone tried working around this issue by using custom fetch adapter?

Since [Axios now allows passing custom fetch adapter](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/7003), maybe this can be resolved with using [Undici fetch](https://undici.nodejs.org/#/?id=undicifetchinput-init-promise) which is a ["relaxed" version](https://undici.nodejs.org/#/?id=specification-compliance) of Node fetch.

Error [ERR_REQUIRE_ESM]: Must use import to load ES Module: <path>\src\axios-1.6.2\index.js
require() of ES modules is not supported.
require() of <path>\src\axios-1.6.2\index.js from <path>\src\api\index.api.ts is an ES module file as it is a .js file whose nearest parent package.json contains "type": "module" which defines all .js files in that package scope as ES modules.

body: You're trying to do something that is not supported or encouraged really. npm packages are supposed to be npm install-ed. the issue you're facing is that you're importing the source code of axios, instead of the built code. because the source code is written in ESM, you cannot "require" it from a CommonsJS codebase.

author: Stun3R

body:
Hi Stun3R,

I am afraid situation is getting worse... the newest (fixed version) has even more lines of code that crashes if ever you defined global "window" and "document" variables to object BEFORE loading axios.

This is in fact perfectly natural as "Axios" considers that if those two are defined as objects
 then it is being loaded in a browser...  which is unfortunately not true in our case.

Unfortunately again, I am still forced to do those two defines to satisfy some other old libraries I am still using.

The easy work around I am now using involved locating where in my code were the two global definitions done,
 and then simply enforce loading "axios" in advance BEFORE defining the two "window" and "document" globals.

Best regards.
author: Bozzzo

body:
Hi DigitalBrainJS,

I am afraid fix is not sufficient... you still have lots of lines that are conditioned on hasBrowserEnv or hasStandardBrowserEnv
that would crash if window & document are not what they are supposed to be (full functional window and document).
For example the two simple tests
Ex :
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/144731df-fd53-40ae-b55d-87045a1721f7)

But I think this is not up to you to fix the consequence of us breaking environment by defining those two globals.

Besides, the fact that hasStandardBrowserEnv and/or hasBrowserEnv are true when those globals are set,
 might cause unwanted change in behavior as per what we should get when running axios in bare NodeJS without them.

I mean that even if all the potential crashes are protected by testing before every access to every member of window & document ; we would still get unwanted behavior... basically because we lied on presence of window & document and hasBrowserEnv and hasStandardBrowserEnv should still be false because they are not real window & document.

But may be someone knows some better way to check for real window and document ...

Yet in fact it would be better not to have to lure other libs with fake window & document in the first place this is too dangerous I will try to get rid of this.

In the meantime I'll be satisfied with my work-around to load axios in advance

Best regard, and cheers

body:
Hey @RubenVerborgh ! Thanks for the notice! 
However, Axios doesn't have follow-redirects in the browser build. It's only used in HTTP adapter module, which is shadowed by a null value depending on the browser & conditional export fields in the package.json. So this issue can only happen if the user makes his build with the wrong bundler/pipeline config.

body:
### Troubleshooting Network Error after Upgrading to Expo SDK 50 in iOS (only)

If you are encountering network errors after upgrading to Expo SDK 50, particularly when using an HTTP URL, follow these steps to resolve the issue:

1. Open the `info.plist` file within the `iOS` directory of your Expo project.

2. Add the following lines to the `info.plist` file under  `<key>NSAppTransportSecurity</key>`

```xml
<key>NSAllowsArbitraryLoads</key>
<true/>
<key>NSExceptionDomains</key>
<dict>
  <key>localhost</key>
  <dict>
    <key>NSExceptionAllowsInsecureHTTPLoads</key>
    <true/>
  </dict>
</dict>

body:
axios sets content type for POST to `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` by default.
Explicitly providing
```javascript
headers: {

body:
Hello!

I have not been able to reproduce your problem. Reproducing your environment made sure that everything works as expected. It seems that in your situation the problem is not with axios library, but with import. Did you specify the following line in pacakge.json:
`"type": "module"` 
Also it can be mistake in your esm configuration.
author: msugenius

body:
Hi,

In package.json `"type": "module"` is not specified. 
This is because the code works a little differently. (I feel like I should have shared this with you before)

in **package.json**, is works as below
`"main": "src/index.js"`

and `index.js` has below code. 
```
require = require("esm")(module);
module.exports = require("./main.js")
```

finally, `main.js` uses example code above
```
// This work with 0.27.2 but not work with 1.6.7
// as change import axios from 'axios';
import http from 'axios';
...
const res = await http.head(url, {timeout: 1000})
...
```

I think this confusing caused by mixing `require` and `import` in both `index.js` and `main.js`.

I searched and considering to re-write all code as ESM, which may be better solution for this.

Thanks again for advice @msugenius
author: jhk0530

body:
Hi!

Thanks for the additional code snippets, now I realize that my conclusion was hasty. Those module are really incompatible since of `axios@1.0.0`. My recommendations are as follows:
1) Try to avoid using `esm` (the module has not been updated for 5 years and therefore may not be compatible with other modern modules and node versions) and use `"type":" module"` along with it
2) If the first option is impossible you can still use `axios@0.27.2`.

I hope my answer was useful.

body:
I found that axios has a `lib/defaults/transitional.js` to guide its code parse response text to json forcely when `responseType` is undefined.

The default response parse function:

https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/a9f47afbf3224d2ca987dbd8188789c7ea853c5d/lib/defaults/index.js#L100-L126

The transitional configuration:

https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/a9f47afbf3224d2ca987dbd8188789c7ea853c5d/lib/defaults/transitional.js#L1-L7

So axios response json object default , when we don't pass `responseType` in axios options parameters.

author: IrinaLuminesk

body: Browsers do not expose `Set-Cookie` to frontend JavaScript response headers, even when other headers are exposed. Cookies should be handled by the browser cookie jar with the correct server CORS and credential settings, not by reading `Set-Cookie` through Axios. This is a browser security model limitation rather than an Axios bug, so I am closing this.
