author: kriskowal

body:
IMHO I doubt we will adhere to the concept of "Hardened JS". It will complicate the current and future code, and Object.defineProperty has certain performance costs compared to simple assignment, which might not be acceptable somewhere. The costs will affect everyone, while few use this "super strict mode". Even if we "fix" it now, then we will be driven into the framework of this concept in further development, and simply due to an edge case for a few users.
The limitations need to be examined more closely to predict the potential complications, but it's not entirely clear what practical problems it solves that justify the codebase complication, other than the desire to run untrusted eval in a non-separate worker.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
This is a very understandable position and I respect it.

The interesting case in composition with Axios is merely isolating third-party dependencies to mitigate some flavors of prototype pollution and supply chain attacks, as with http://github.com/lavamoat/lavamoat. There are also perhaps a minority of cases where Axios participates in the kernel-mode side of a platform that executes a plugin in the same process.

Regarding performance, the above patch touches only the runtime for class definition and looks a lot like code generated by [TypeScript to downgrade class syntax to ES5 semantics](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/43450). From many years watching similar issues, I don’t expect this to be a slippery slope into a deep maintenance burden.

For your consideration.

body:
I've similarly observed a breaking change that I believe is related and was not included in the CHANGELOG.

Specifically, before `0.28.0`, if I have a querystring like:

```
const qs = {
    foo: 1,
    bar: 'xyz',
    baz: { }
};
```

When passed to axios.get – like `axios.get("https://some.uri", { params: qs })` – the resulting query params that are passed to `https://some.uri` in the constructed request are:

```
foo=1&bar=xyz&baz=%7B%7D
```

In 0.28.0, that same request yields the following querystring params (_excluding the `baz` param entirely_):

```
foo=1&bar=xyz
```

---

This is a seemingly understandable optimization, but is in fact a breaking change for anyone who relies on that functionality to remain as-is.

body:
It doesn't seem like this is an Axios issue. I've never seen this behavior in RFC or issues related to this. But in any case, this is exactly how the browsers’ network API acts (fetch&XMLHttpRequest), so it looks like Rails will have problems with communication with the frontend. Axios for the browser relies entirely on the browser API to work with FormData, it does not have its own FormData encoder, unlike Axios for Node.js. However, it implements the same behavior.

```js
await (await fetch('https://httpbin.org/post', {method: 'post', body: new FormData()})).json()
```

```
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: localhost:3000
Connection: keep-alive
Content-Length: 44
Pragma: no-cache
Cache-Control: no-cache
sec-ch-ua: "Chromium";v="122", "Not(A:Brand";v="24", "Google Chrome";v="122"
sec-ch-ua-platform: "Windows"
sec-ch-ua-mobile: ?0
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/122.0.0.0 Safari/537.36
Content-Type: multipart/form-data; boundary=----WebKitFormBoundaryvXB63Fbymccy21ky
Accept: */*
Origin: https://axios-http.com
Sec-Fetch-Site: cross-site
Sec-Fetch-Mode: cors
Sec-Fetch-Dest: empty
Accept-Encoding: gzip, deflate, br, zstd
Accept-Language: uk,uk-UA;q=0.9,en-US;q=0.8,en;q=0.7,ru-UA;q=0.6,ru-RU;q=0.5,ru;q=0.4

------WebKitFormBoundaryvXB63Fbymccy21ky--
```
![image](https://github.com/axios/axios/assets/12586868/8643099d-3ea5-4d26-b1ec-e2484eb29d11)
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Interesting. I agree, this is not an Axios issue and we can close this ticket. 
That being said, the RFC is pretty clear:

> The body must then contain
>    **one or more body parts**, each preceded by a boundary delimiter line,
>    and the last one followed by a closing boundary delimiter line.

This is from the old [rfc2046](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2046#section-5.1)  pointed as the source of truth for the definition of multipart form data in [the current rfc7578](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7578#section-4)

This suggest that Rack (therefore Rails) follows the spec, but perhaps a bit too rigidly. The spec does warn against being too rigid in regard to field names for example.

Seems like it would fall on the browser to not send this request (or make it valid somehow), but I feel like the spec is overly restrictive here and should allow for an empty body (with a final boundary).

I'll start a discussion in the Rack issue tracker. Perhaps they'd be open to relax the parsing a bit.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
Hello,

I spent days searching for the issue, but I finally found the cause!
In my case, it turned out to be a network problem, not a React Native issue.

1. IPTABLES Rule Blocking TCP Requests

I had an IPTABLES rule limiting the number of TCP calls:

iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80  -m limit --limit 10/minute --limit-burst 20 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -m limit --limit 10/minute --limit-burst 20 -j ACCEPT


I replaced it with:

iptables -R INPUT 10 -p tcp --dport 443 \
  -m hashlimit --hashlimit-name https-per-ip \
  --hashlimit 50/min --hashlimit-burst 100 \
  --hashlimit-mode srcip --hashlimit-srcmask 32 \
  -j ACCEPT

iptables -R INPUT 9 -p tcp --dport 80 \
  -m hashlimit --hashlimit-name http-per-ip \
  --hashlimit 50/min --hashlimit-burst 100 \
  --hashlimit-mode srcip --hashlimit-srcmask 32 \
  -j ACCEPT

2. Nginx Rate Limiting in Backend Configuration

I also had an Nginx rate-limit directive in my backend config:

limit_req zone=api burst=5 nodelay;

I replaced it with:

limit_req_zone $binary_remote_addr zone=api:10m rate=30r/m;


You can adjust these values depending on your needs.

3. How to Debug This Kind of Issue?

- Don’t rely only on React Native logs.
- Start by tracing logs from end to end.
- Check ADB logs with your phone connected in USB developer mode.
- Then inspect Nginx logs.
- Finally check iptables logs (sometimes noisy due to bots).


I hope this helps others who might run into the same problem.

### Test 2
```html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<body>
<script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/axios@1.6.8/dist/axios.min.js"></script>
<script>
  // Make a GET request to a URL
  axios.get('https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1')
    .then(function (response) {
      // Handle success
      console.log('Response:', response.data);
    })
    .catch(function (error) {
      // Handle error
      console.error('Error:', error);
    });
</script>
</body>
</html>
```

This does not work on chromium 38, but does work on firefox 123.

### Conclusion
Although neither of these code samples work with chromium 38, they also don't work with any version of axios. It seems as though axios has never worked with chromium 38.

body: For anybody trying to look at this issue, content length is calculated in [```lib/adapters/http.js```](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/lib/adapters/http.js).
author: justindhillon

body:
@angelalawrence123 , this is a pretty big diffrence between postman and axios. You should make sure that everything is good on your side:

1.  Ensure that the content of the request body is identical between Postman and Axios. Even small differences in payload content, such as whitespace or encoding, can result in different byte lengths.

2. Check that both Postman and Axios are serializing the request body in the same format. For example, if you're sending JSON data, make sure that the serialization process is consistent and produces the same result in both environments.

3.  Verify that the Content-Type header and any other relevant headers are identical between Postman and Axios.

4. Ensure that character encoding settings are consistent between Postman and Axios. Encoding differences can lead to variations in byte length, especially for non-ASCII characters.

If you have snippets of your code, I will have an easier time diagnosing your problem.

body:
I have tried everything with axios to get this to work... It does not.
I tried 4 different agents including the tunnel httpsOverHttp for my use case and in all cases it fails.
I have gotten the native https module to work fine, but this requires parsing the payload. When no proxy is in place, the key/cert/passphrase on the httpsAgent works fine, as soon as a proxy config is added, the call is proxied correctly, but the SSL cert/key/passphrase are dropped.
So in my case, Axios call hits proxy (no auth) and in turn proxy hits server requiring ssl, and throws error due to ssl not being present.

body: This is linked to the HTTPS-over-HTTP proxy work. Current v1.x now installs a tunnelling agent for HTTPS targets through HTTP proxies and forwards user TLS options, including custom CA-related agent options, into that tunnel path. I am going to close this as likely fixed. If a current v1.x reproduction remains, please open a new issue with a minimal proxy/CA setup.

author: Vringe

body:
This is not a problem with [`axios`](https://github.com/axios/axios), this is a problem with [`node`](https://github.com/nodejs/node). 

Axios relies on node's build in `globalAgent` for http and https. https_proxy, http_proxy, and no_proxy are all handled by node, not axios.

If you want this to be resolved, you can make an issue at [`node`](https://github.com/nodejs/node).

author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
We've been hampered by this issue for months and are stuck on node v18.18.1 because of it. We don't use axios directly in our project, but the [@slack/web-api](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@slack/web-api) and [stream-chat](https://www.npmjs.com/package/stream-chat) libraries that we use in our project both use axios. I have no idea if axios is the real source of the issue here, but the error occurs anytime those libraries try to make an http request on node v18.18.2 or higher via axios.

Our "fix" is to downgrade to node v18.18.1. As @mattwatts-aimltd-uk says, anything v18.18.2 or greater gets this same error.

author: Vringe

body:
This is not a problem with [`axios`](https://github.com/axios/axios), this is a problem with [`node`](https://github.com/nodejs/node). 

Axios relies on node's build in `globalAgent` for http and https. https_proxy, http_proxy, and no_proxy are all handled by node, not axios.

If you want this to be resolved, you can make an issue at [`node`](https://github.com/nodejs/node).

body:
@yuchen001 
I was having the same problem.
Returning 204status would result in a pending status on the client side.

I was returning the following from the backend
```
return Response({}, status=status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT)
```
Then I did the following and the client worked fine.
```
return Response(status=status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT)
```

Not sure why it was working with previous axios.

body:
We've been hampered by this issue for months and are stuck on node v18.18.1 because of it. We don't use axios directly in our project, but the [@slack/web-api](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@slack/web-api) and [stream-chat](https://www.npmjs.com/package/stream-chat) libraries that we use in our project both use axios. I have no idea if axios is the real source of the issue here, but the error occurs anytime those libraries try to make an http request on node v18.18.2 or higher via axios.

Our "fix" is to downgrade to node v18.18.1. As @mattwatts-aimltd-uk says, anything v18.18.2 or greater gets this same error.

body:
Thank you for your response. I don't have CVE link but have some data from fortify scan. The file path in the repo is \axios\dist\axios.js

In this case, the data is sent at setAttribute() in axios.js file.

The malicious content sent to the web browser often takes the form of a JavaScript segment, but can also include HTML, Flash or any other type of code that the browser executes. The variety of attacks based on XSS is almost limitless, but they commonly include transmitting private data such as cookies or other session information to the attacker, redirecting the victim to web content controlled by the attacker, or performing other malicious operations on the user's machine under the guise of the vulnerable site.
Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities occur when:

1. Data enters a web application through an untrusted source. In the case of DOM-based XSS, data is read from a URL parameter or other value within the browser and written back into the page with client-side code. In the case of reflected XSS, the untrusted source is typically a web request, while in the case of persisted (also known as stored) XSS it is typically a database or other back-end data store.


2. The data is included in dynamic content that is sent to a web user without validation. In the case of DOM-based XSS, malicious content is executed as part of DOM (Document Object Model) creation, whenever the victim's browser parses the HTML page.

author: scku208

body:
There is no security vulnerability here.  The code is contained wholly in the `isURLSameOrigin` code.  That code simply compares two urls and returns a boolean if their origin is the same.  The url objects created therein that have their `href` attributes set are never returned, and thus couldn't possibly be serialized or displayed to the end user.

The PR addressing this is generally a good code cleanup, but it's just that.  No CVE should be made from this and folks who are following along should not concern themselves about a security issue here.

body: It would generally help the security of Javascript and Node.js if folks would actually be required to show a vulnerability being made, rather than point out a line of code that does something suspect.  ChatGPT is not a valid security researcher.

body: I also don't think this would cause security attacks unless someone can provide a POC.

body:
I also experience the same issue, although in my case this issue occuer only when the `data` object have a property with the key `query` and the value is a string

For example 

This produce the error:
```
axios.request({
        method: "POST",
        URL: "some-url",
        headers: {  'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        data: { query: "any value" },
    })
```

This does not produce the error:
```
axios.request({
        method: "POST",
        url: "some-url",
        headers: {  'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
        data: { notQuery: "any value" },
    })
```

The error:
`AppError [TypeError]: Converting circular structure to JSON
    --> starting at object with constructor 'ClientRequest'
    |     property 'socket' -> object with constructor 'TLSSocket'
    --- property '_httpMessage' closes the circle`


- axios version: 1.6.8
- enviroment: node.js
- node version: v18.17.1
- npm version: 9.6.7
- system: macOS 14.4 (23E214)

Simply don't throw an error if the request succeeded.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
Yes, it wouldn't be easy. Errors causes are not standardized, they are different in browser/node(undici/cross-fetch/etc)/ReactNative.
Writing an error parser for each platform can hardly be called an acceptable solution, especially since there are no guarantees that there are no differences between versions. For now, I'm inclined to consider `TypeError: Failed to fetch` as an AxiosError with ERR_NETWORK code. Maybe someone will come up with a better solution.

P.S. Thanks for the test example & repo. It's rare to see such a well-filled issue. ❤️

body: this is a known issue with error handling in RN, not specific to axios. axios creates the error internally, and when catched the the original call site stack is lost. underlying issue is that Rn error handling (especially with hermes) doesn't preserve stack traces the same way Node.js does

body: I checked the current v1.x behaviour, and Axios does not strip arbitrary custom headers just because their name contains `proxy`. The Node adapter sends headers such as `proxy_token` and other custom names normally. Axios only treats `Proxy-Authorization` specially. Browser restrictions around forbidden request headers are platform behaviour, not Axios behaviour. Closing as not reproducible in Axios.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
Hi @meghnasamoorthy, apologies for the slow response on this one, it has been sitting in the backlog longer than it should have.

A few things before we can dig in further. The report is against 1.6.2 and we are now on 1.16.0, with quite a bit of CJS/ESM interop work having landed in between. The current `exports` map resolves browser plus `require` to `dist/browser/axios.cjs`, and that bundle exports the axios instance with `.default` self-pointing, so `require('axios').default.get(...)` should work today. Can you confirm whether you still see the same failure on 1.16.0, and if so, share a minimal reproduction repo I can clone and run. Without that I do not have enough to act on.

While I am here, two things worth flagging that may be in play:

1. The browser-globals branch of your UMD wrapper does `factory(root)`, where every other branch passes `{ axios: ... }`. If that branch ever runs, `imports.axios.get` resolves against `window` and you get exactly the "axios.get is not a function" error, regardless of the axios version. Worth checking which branch is actually being hit in your bundle, the AMD/CJS branches or the globals fallback.

2. react-scripts has been deprecated by Meta, so its webpack config is not something we can target directly, even if there is a real interop wrinkle there. If you have the flexibility to move to Vite or a direct Webpack 5 setup, that would also help us narrow down whether the issue is with Axios or the CRA build pipeline.

I will close this as stale, but happy to reopen the moment there is a fresh repro to look at.

body:
Like you said, the Fetch API specification defines both the second argument of `fetch` *and* the second constructor parameter of `Request` to be a `RequestInit`. Axios has a `proxy` config field which applies to the `http` adapter on Node. So if there's not a specific reason you need to use the Fetch API, that's an option.

But, otherwise, axios doesn't provide proxy support and you'll have to wait for it to be exposed upstream ([1](https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/42814), [2](https://github.com/nodejs/node/issues/43187)). It's unlikely they'll add non-standard workarounds for proxying on a single platform.

body:
Having problems with "Favorite" functionality throwing "Not Signed In" error, It is because I have a newer version of Next & NextAuth,
a small modification is needed (you can find it in the github repostory). Here are the steps:
1.  [...nextauth].ts file should export authOptions separately
2.  serverAuth.ts file should use getServerSession(req, res, authOptions) instead of getSession({req})
3. Modify serverAuth(req) to serverAuth(req, res) everywhere in your code.
4. Logout, shutdown the app, login again, everything should work

> Having problems with "Favorite" functionality throwing "Not Signed In" error, It is because I have a newer version of Next & NextAuth, a small modification is needed (you can find it in the github repostory). Here are the steps:
> 
> 1. [...nextauth].ts file should export authOptions separately
> 2. serverAuth.ts file should use getServerSession(req, res, authOptions) instead of getSession({req})
> 3. Modify serverAuth(req) to serverAuth(req, res) everywhere in your code.
> 4. Logout, shutdown the app, login again, everything should work

Thanks a ton!

body:
Closing, this error is thrown by WebKit's fetch implementation when it encounters a body type it doesn't recognise (typically a React Native-style `{ uri, type, name }` blob passed through a web fetch path, or a FormData containing an unsupported entry).

It is not something axios can fix — the body is handed to the platform fetch/XHR as-is. Workarounds: convert the body to a Blob or an ArrayBuffer before passing it to axios, or use the Node adapter if running in a non-browser context.

body: With further debugging, we found that the issue was happening whenever the newer Axios error object was being logged by the logger. When the logger was trying to log the entire error object it was causing memory spikes due to circular json. We fixed this by json stringifying the error object before passing to the logger.

author: pinussilvestrus

body:
Hi. Trying to understand this vulnerability better.

**Mixed use of relative and absolute (including, protocol-relative) URLs**

Axios will see `//google.com` as an argument to `get` call, since the text substitution will occur outside of the function call and that's an acceptable value, isn't it? It's quite possible that the same client instance could be used for fetching both relative URL and absolute URL based resources.
```
userId = '/google.com'
this.axios.get(`/${userId}`);
```

Of course this may not align with application developer's expectation (ie. to be relative path). But it feels like that's not Axios' problem to solve. Axios could potentially warn this use pattern (ie. use of baseUrl with non relative path), but it seems too harsh for axios to dictate this.

**Impact of using protocol-relative URLs**

The following code kinda forces `http` protocol if protocol-relative URLs are used. 
```
const parsed = new URL(fullPath, 'http://localhost');
```

Is this the main concern here? I guess an attacker could potentially move away from non-http protocol (eg. local file location, deemed safe) to http protocol to deliver remotely located malicious files into the target system.

**Expected behavior**

>An error could be raised when attempting to request protocol-relative URLs server-side as unlike in a client-side browser session, there is no established protocol to be relative to.

If this is indeed a good solution, then `isAbsoluteURL` could be updated to ignore protocol-relative URLs to produce this output. Please note `config.url` is set by the `_request` [implementation,](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/c6cce43cd94489f655f4488c5a50ecaf781c94f2/lib/core/Axios.js#L65), so it will be still `//google.com`. But requested url will be **combined** url.

```
config.baseURL:  https://userapi.example.com
config.method:   get
config.url:      //google.com
res.responseUrl: https://userapi.example.com//google.com
```

author: rrkoshta123

body:
> Axios's (current) baseURL behavior seems like a similar behavior of `src=` tags of a browser. Which is one possible, and (IMO) well defined design. (not saying "[in]correct"|"good"/"bad")
> 
> ...
> 
> Same Vuln report would apply to (the design of?) `fetch` of browsers?

The problem here isn't necessarily the behaviour of axios in the browser, it's the behaviour of it in a server-side context where the server is making requests from a private network.

As the report says, in a server-side context, there is no base protocol for a protocol-relative URL to be relative to. Therefore, a protocol-relative URL should not be considered valid in that context.

How the attack could be constructed is sort of secondary to that. If it doesn't make sense in that context, we should disallow it.

body:
### Proposed Solution

The root cause is the lack of fuzzing integration in the axios repository, making it vulnerable to potential security issues. To address this, we need to integrate Google's OSS-Fuzz into the axios project. This will help identify and capture security vulnerabilities.

Implementation plan:
1. Create a new directory `fuzz` in the root of the repository.
2. Add a `fuzz.js` file with the fuzzing logic, using the `lib/axios.js` file as the entry point.
3. Configure OSS-Fuzz by creating a `oss-fuzz.yaml` file in the root directory.

Code changes:
```javascript
// fuzz/fuzz.js
const axios = require('../lib/axios');

// Fuzzing logic here
```
```yml
# oss-fuzz.yaml
projects:
  - name: axios
    build:
      - make build
    fuzz:
      - fuzz.js
```
Testing suggestions: Run `make fuzz` to test the fuzzing integration, and verify that it's working as expected by checking the OSS-Fuzz dashboard.

---
<sub>Happy to submit a PR implementing this approach if it looks good to the maintainers.</sub>

body:
No. Neither Axios nor Node’s https.Agent provide a way to require that the server request a client certificate or to fail the connection if it doesn’t. This behaviour is governed by the TLS handshake, and Node.js doesn’t expose a control to change it.

Axios itself has no special mTLS logic; all behaviour depends on Node.js’s https.Agent and the TLS protocol. The client cannot force the server to request or require a certificate; that must be enforced on the server side or checked via custom logic on the client.

author: JoepKockelkorn

body:
After literally light‑years of debugging I finally discovered that Android’s OkHttp **will not** fetch missing intermediates the way iOS or desktop browsers do. My server was only ever serving the leaf certificate, so every HTTPS call on Android instantly failed with `ERR_NETWORK`.

**The fix is 100% Nginx‑side**—no application‑level hacks needed:

1. **Build a true full‑chain bundle**  
   Concatenate your leaf cert plus all intermediate certs into one file:
 
```bash
   cat /etc/nginx/ssl/<YOUR_DOMAIN>.crt \
       /etc/nginx/ssl/<YOUR_DOMAIN>.ca-bundle \
     > /etc/nginx/ssl/<YOUR_DOMAIN>_fullchain.crt
```


Verify you now have two or more certificates in it:

```bash
grep -c "BEGIN CERTIFICATE" /etc/nginx/ssl/<YOUR_DOMAIN>_fullchain.crt
# → should print 2+
```

2. **Point Nginx at the full‑chain**
   In your SSL server block, reference the new bundle:

   ```nginx
   server {
     listen 443 ssl;
     server_name <YOUR_DOMAIN> www.<YOUR_DOMAIN>;

     ssl_certificate     /etc/nginx/ssl/<YOUR_DOMAIN>_fullchain.crt;
     ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/ssl/<YOUR_DOMAIN>.key;

     # …your proxy_pass and other config…
   }
   ```

3. **Test & reload Nginx**

   ```bash
   nginx -t && nginx -s reload
   ```

4. **Confirm the chain**

   ```bash
   openssl s_client -connect <YOUR_DOMAIN>:443 -showcerts
   ```

   You should see both your leaf **and** intermediate(s) listed, ending with:

   ```
   Verify return code: 0 (ok)
   ```

Once Android sees the complete chain, OkHttp will happily complete the TLS handshake and your HTTPS requests will succeed on **both** iOS and Android.

```
::contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}
```

body:
I agree with this, the current implementation to revert the encoding of some characters is indeed surprising, and also not documented. IIRC the characters `[` and `]` are considered unsafe and must be escaped.

Current logic (https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.x/lib/helpers/buildURL.js):
```
return encodeURIComponent(val).
    replace(/%3A/gi, ':').
    replace(/%24/g, '$').
    replace(/%2C/gi, ',').
    replace(/%20/g, '+').
    replace(/%5B/gi, '[').
    replace(/%5D/gi, ']');
```

body:
This was done intentionally as it was assumed that simple config normalization would not introduce any real breaking changes, however, the decision is debatable. On the one hand, we have the initial config, on the other, we have the final config with which the request was executed. Both approaches have pros and cons.
If this leads to incompatibilities, it should be considered a regression bug and fixed for the current major version. It depends on community feedback.

author: gepbird

body: @DigitalBrainJS is there a way to turn off the throttling introduced in #6371?
author: gepbird

body:
@gepbird No, although adding such an option is trivial. Are there any reasons why we need it?
I can’t find the related github issue, but someone asked to limit the firing of progress events, since this leads to frequent changes in the UI state and, accordingly, redrawing of components.
That sounds reasonable as smooth progress bar raising can be achieved with simple CSS animation and triggering an event every 20ms is not necessary.
But if the community wants to see an option that regulates or completely disables throttling for xhr, then I see no problem adding it.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
@DigitalBrainJS thanks for your quick response!

The reason why disabling throttling would be useful is the user could see a more up-to-date progress bar. Yes, it is only a few milliseconds more recent information, but in my opinion it improves the user experience by a noticeable amount. On the following video I used axios 1.7.3, top progress bar had a 0.2s transition (it was smooth on 1.6.8), bottom progress bar had a 0.5s transition:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/267ce0be-65a8-44b3-a82c-a7438b8fcfb6

The CSS for smoothing the progress bar: `transition: width ease-out 0.2s, background-color ease-out 0.2s;`.

I'd love to see an option where the throttling can be turned off, but I understand if it's low priority since turned out this is not a bug in axios.

author: jmthomas

body:
Taking a quick look, seems https://github.com/axios/axios/commit/e3c76fc9bdd03aa4d98afaf211df943e2031453f#diff-2175f7ecc915e8b17c9831b9bbcd0a681759f7227a424964b04ab68e8166dd77L157-R160 modified a check from:

```js
if (typeof _config.onUploadProgress === 'function' && request.upload) {
```

... to:

```js
if (onUploadProgress && request.upload) {
```

The previous `typeof` seems to have silently (accidentally?) filtered out invalid configs, while the new check would allow passing anything as long as it is truthy.

If somewhere in your code you're using a config with an `onUploadProgress` that is not a function, this would explain why `progressEventReducer` is receiving a `listener` that's not a function, causing the error.

That would be my guess.
author: c032

body: @c032 Thanks for the suggestion! We are indeed passing an empty object `{}` to onUploadProgress by default and then only setting it to a function if we need that functionality. I'll set it to `false` by default instead.

body:
I can confirm I have the same issue here.  If I run version 1.7.2, I do not have the issue.  When I update to 1.7.3 (because of the high vulnerability fix), I then cannot build the app.  The following error in my case:

Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'process/browser' in '..../node_modules/axios/lib'
Did you mean 'browser.js'?
BREAKING CHANGE: The request 'process/browser' failed to resolve only because it was resolved as fully specified
(probably because the origin is strict EcmaScript Module, e. g. a module with javascript mimetype, a '*.mjs' file, or a '*.js' file where the package.json contains '"type": "module"').
The extension in the request is mandatory for it to be fully specified.
Add the extension to the request.

My `config-overrides.js` has aways had :

```
    new webpack.ProvidePlugin({
      process: 'process/browser'
    })
```

@filoucrackeur - Curious if you have since found any workarounds.
author: mikeCapozziICF

body: @mikeCapozziICF my workaround ----> downgrade version of axios to         "axios": "^1.6.8",
author: filoucrackeur

body:
Webpack is probably trying to import shim for mocking `process` global, which is incorrect since there is no actual usage of this variable in the code on the browser platform- only a check for the existence of the variable to determine the platform type. This is most likely a bug or misconfiguration of webpack.
https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/ee208cfcae8770ac579f26e7278867567886e026/lib/utils.js#L698

Any development should strive to deliver product improvements to the end user with a minimum delay, this is the essence of Agile, so without a boot screen like "Working on Axios updates. Don't turn off your PC." it's hard to imagine what could be "really annoying" about updates.

But whatever, I'll just remove Axios from all my products, problem solved.

body:
That sort of defeats the purpose of an "instance". I would rather configure an instance to have Basic Auth and override it when JWT's are relevant.
Sure I could encode the basic auth credentials myself directly into the Authorization header but that defeats the purpose of `auth`.

On the other hand if I avoid using the instance to pre-configure the basic auth in `auth` then every separate request from that instance needs to inject it, which is just a nuisance but entirely avoidable.
author: karloluis

body:
To address your comment directly. A bit nuanced but hopefully it helps shed light on what I think caused issues for me.

The docs state this auth vs header without distinction between options in a request and those based on a pre-configured instance. Since the instance obfuscates the use of `auth` it is reasonable to assume that it would cause confusion. 
> ie. "I did not state `auth` in the `.post('/', opts)` options, but rather a JWT. Why am I seeing something else?" Which is caused by the instance declared potentially somewhere else.

body:
> Is Axios checking the robots.txt file

No of course. Axios is a request library, not a search robot. It would be strange if Axios, fetch, or curl checked robots.txt before making a request. The user can make any requests from their server to your backend, including replacing the user-agent signature. Protection from unscrupulous clients lies entirely with the backend developers.

body: It's here https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/5b8a826771b77ab30081d033fdba9ef3b90e439a/lib/helpers/resolveConfig.js#L28-L31
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/v1.7.2/lib/defaults/index.js#L53
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a3aff807-aec8-44d1-94ad-661c36110a1d)
Why is data of type formData converted to JOSN string in index.js under the default directory. Is this mutually exclusive with utilis.isFormData in resolveConfig
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5a2569cf-138c-4d74-95d4-4eb1885c2199)
Due to certain historical reasons, the default content type is application/json. In version 0. x, the data type is formData, and Axios sets the content type to null. However, in version 1. x, the content type type is prioritized. What are the benefits of this? Why not place this judgment after isFormData
version: 1.7.2

They aren't mutually exclusive. They are not related to each other in any way. This conversion only applies to requests with an explicit content-type set to `application/json`. The valid content type for FormData is `multipart/form-data`, so if the user sets the content type to json for FormData, the form will be serialized to JSON.
The default request content type is `application/x-www-form-urlencoded` for both major versions. In addition, Axios has no default content-type for the response, by default, it just assumes the presence of JSON in the response and tries to parse it despite its content-type header.

body:
I think we hit a related issue.

After updating from axios 1.7.2 to 1.7.7 we started having an issue. The program flow in our application, during error handling on a certain POST request, is controlled by the message in the error response. But in 1.7.7 the message in the error is not exposed!

**With axios 1.7.2 we get an error object that looks like this:**

Object.keys(error) returns the array `['message', 'name', 'code', 'config', 'request', 'response']`, and the error is an instance of AxiosError.

**After updating to axios 1.7.7:**

The error object has no fields (Object.keys(error) returns an empty array). The error is an instance of Error, NOT AxiosError. There are no "response"-field.


So yes, we do lack the response-field in the error thrown during axios.post, and this is causing a serious issue in our application. Luckily we noticed before going to production.

body:
I managed to make a minimal reproduction that shows how the bug showed up in our application. The fault is probably our own, in our "mapApiError"-method. It looks for a "error.status" field, and if it doesn't exist, the mapper return the error itself.

In axios 1.7.7, the error.status field shows up, and our mapper kicks in!

Perhaps this is equivalent with the observed behaviour @IftekharHakimK reports?

Minimal reproduction here, you can see the different behaviour if you change between axios 1.7.2 and axios 1.7.7 in package.json:


https://codesandbox.io/p/devbox/issue-6596-forked-vj42v7

body:
Closing, this is expected. When the browser blocks a response due to CORS (missing Access-Control-Allow-Origin), the JS layer receives an opaque network error with no status code, even though the network panel shows the actual response code. Axios surfaces what the browser exposes.

Fix this on the server by returning the correct CORS headers (including on error responses). Not an axios bug.

body:
Opened PR: https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/10930

Fixes `TypeError [ERR_INVALID_URL]` for Unix socket requests introduced in `1.7.4`, where `new URL(fullPath, ...)` rejected path-only URLs such as `/foo` when used with `socketPath`.
This restores the `1.7.3` behavior by supplying a synthetic `http://localhost` base only for `socketPath` requests.
Non-socket requests remain unchanged, and absolute URLs continue to behave per the URL specification.

Happy if this helps 🙂

As per docs referred, direct access to headers is decrypted, therefor `r.config.headers["X-XSRF-TOKEN"]` won't work.
You will have to iterate over the headers to obtain the key you are looking for.

*Note*, maybe you would be able access the header using the get method on `AxiosHeaders` using `request.headers.get("X-XSRF-TOKEN")`

body:
Closing as this is already solved in the current code base:

- AxiosInstance includes create(config?: CreateAxiosDefaults): AxiosInstance in both index.d.ts:703 and index.d.cts:630.
- Because the parameter is optional, axios.create().create() is valid in TypeScript.
- Runtime also supports it via instance.create(...) in lib/axios.js:39.

body: @panta82 if there are multiple headers with same key, should the ideal behavior not be to overwrite them, except for 'set-cookie' which should create a list?
author: anchor54

body:
In node.js, I think the rule is:

for all headers:
- for the first occurance -> set as `'h1=v1'`
- for the second occurrence -> convert to `['h1=v1', 'h2=v2']`

for `set-cookie`:
- for the first occurrence -> set as `['h1=v1']`
- for the second occurrence -> append to existing list `['h1=v1', 'h2=v2']`

More info: https://nodejs.org/api/http.html#messageheaders

body:
yes it should be  method?: Method | (string & {}).
Method | string: This allows Method values (like 'GET', 'POST', etc.) or any string to be assigned to the method field.

Method | (string & {}): This ensures that Method values are allowed, but for other strings, it requires them to be explicitly typed as strings with no additional properties. The string & {} forces TypeScript to treat arbitrary strings in a stricter way, often to avoid loose string handling in union types.

body:
This is going to take longer than I thought because I remembered why I didn't add this earlier in v1, even though I planned to. Currently, the fetch adapter includes static checks for API support, as our adapter loader in version 1 is relatively simple. Checking this for every request is not practical because it will impact performance. So, we need caching mechanisms and some improvements to the adapter loader, where we are limited by the inability to make breaking changes. We need to think about this carefully because in the current major version, we can add new functionality, but we cannot change or remove it if an unsuccessful attempt at implementation is made.
There is, of course, a simple implementation option - to create a factory for the fetch adapter, for example adapter: fetch.create({fetch, Request, Response}), but I still tend to use the `env` config to pass fetch, Request, Response objects in order to preserve the axios style in the configuration resolution.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
> we are limited by the inability to make breaking changes

What if you make a new adapter out of the official Axios project ? Since you're an Axios dev, the community would still see it valuable.

> fetch.create({fetch, Request, Response})

If by `Request` you mean [The `Request` interface of the Fetch API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Request), that's actually something you might encounter an additional problem with, as it throws an error when a `url` value without hostname is provided (e.g. just `/foo`), which is essential for internal requests in an SSR context like SvelteKit.

body:
Closing, this is expected behaviour. baseURL is only prepended when the request URL is relative. The relative baseURL itself (e.g., "/api") is resolved against the current document origin in the browser and has no meaningful resolution in Node.

If you want paths joined, use an absolute baseURL (https://example.com/api) and a relative request URL (/users). Please open a new issue with a minimal repro if the behaviour you're seeing differs from this.

body:
This issue does not seem to be a bug; rather, it is expected behavior.

For property inheritance, Axios's `utils.inherits()` assigns properties to the child's `constructor.prototype` using the `Object.assign()` method.

Because `Object.assign()` *assigns* properties, it could throw a TypeError if a property is non-writable, as explained in the [MDN Web Docs](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Object/assign#description). According to your code snippet, you defined `toJSON` as non-writable property, so it's natural for it to throw.

However, Node's `util.inherits()` does not provide functionality that inherits properties by assigning them. It only accepts two parameters: the child and the super constructor.

So on your working example `util.inherits(MyError, Error, { toJSON() { return {} } })`, the third parameter will just be ignored because functions in JavaScript do not throw errors for extra arguments.

Here's the actual code definition in node: [util.js:321](https://github.com/nodejs/node/blob/v20.18.0/lib/util.js#L321)

And here's the code in axios: [utils.js:360](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/main/lib/utils.js#L360)

We have the same issue with **axios v1.8.4** and **TypeScript 5.8.2**. Surprinsingly - in comparision to the issue's title - it was still fine with TypeScript 5.7.3 (and 5.6.3).

The workaround by setting
```
"compilerOptions": {
  "module": "NodeNext",
  "moduleResolution": "NodeNext",
}
```
works in principle but because we use rollup as a bundler and which requires `ES...` as `module`, we cannot go this path.

body:
If you still have this issue after upgrading to node, you might want to make sure [keepAlive](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/1846) is enabled. I had a similar issue where axios was raising ENOMEM errors even though the memory usage never exceeded 500mb (out of 4096mb).

I believe the issue was that during polling operations where the same endpoint is called many times, axios was creating a new TCP connection and for some reason the old ones were not being cleared properly. This was on MacOs.

But my issue fixed by using `AxiosRequestHeaders` instead of `AxiosHeaders`, which is surprising. I am not sure its a right solution way, because `AxiosRequestHeaders` is equals to `RawAxiosRequestHeaders & AxiosHeaders` from the code. But this replacement seems no side-effects to me now. Just search the same error, and it seems like this error will maybe caused by `here is no explicit type annotation on the functions or variables. `, hope can help u to find the true reason of the issue. https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/5711#issuecomment-340283439

> I'm also getting errors when programmatically building code via Jenkins pipeline, and I'm pretty sure the issue is caused from this latest update as well. Every instance of `AxiosHeader` in my code throws an error, and it wasn't doing so before today. ![image](https://private-user-images.githubusercontent.com/103133891/390158843-10410f7a-aa5a-4b53-8f70-9b80e1e6bcaa.png?jwt=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJpc3MiOiJnaXRodWIuY29tIiwiYXVkIjoicmF3LmdpdGh1YnVzZXJjb250ZW50LmNvbSIsImtleSI6ImtleTUiLCJleHAiOjE3MzI3NjAzOTAsIm5iZiI6MTczMjc2MDA5MCwicGF0aCI6Ii8xMDMxMzM4OTEvMzkwMTU4ODQzLTEwNDEwZjdhLWFhNWEtNGI1My04ZjcwLTliODBlMWU2YmNhYS5wbmc_.nobawrauTp9Hgm3a8y2ivhz3dtgjxTlW7tFSTB2xXcM)
> 
> Reverted back to version `1.7.7` (was previously using `^1.4.0` in my `packages.json`) and this resolved the issue for now.

Relace `AxiosHeaders` with `AxiosRequestHeaders` has fixed my issue, detail u can check  [comment](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/6716#issuecomment-2505149884). Hope can help u.

author: jasonsaayman

body:
In the current implementation, the fetch adapter does not call response.json() directly for responseType: 'json'. It reads the payload first, and axios applies JSON parsing later in transformResponse.

If the server returns 500 with an HTML body, the expected default behaviour is that axios still preserves the HTTP response details, so you should be able to inspect:

- error.response.status
- error.response.data

Your best option may be:
- Have a consistent API error format.
- If an endpoint returns non-JSON bodies on error, use responseType: 'text' and parse conditionally.
author: jasonsaayman

body:
The sideEffects: false field in package.json has a specific meaning for bundlers (webpack, rollup, etc.) that's different from the general programming definition of "side effect."

In the bundler context, a "side effect" means code that affects things outside the module's own scope when imported, such as:

- Modifying global variables (e.g., window.foo = bar)
- Polyfilling/modifying built-in prototypes (e.g., Array.prototype.customMethod = ...)
- Making network requests on import
- Writing to files
- Registering global event listeners

What createInstance(defaults) does:

`const axios = createInstance(defaults);`

This creates an Axios instance and assigns it to a module-scoped constant. It doesn't:
- Modify any globals
- Touch the DOM
- Make network requests
- Affect anything outside the module

From the bundler's perspective, this is just internal module initialization. If you import axios but never use any of its exports, a bundler with tree-shaking enabled can safely eliminate the entire module—including the createInstance call—because nothing outside the module depends on that code running.

The distinction:

- FP "side effect": Any operation beyond computing a return value
- Bundler "side effect": Code that has observable effects outside the module scope on import

The axios package correctly uses sideEffects: false because importing it doesn't pollute globals or cause external state changes—it just sets up internal module bindings that are only relevant if you actually use the exports.

body: This happened on our site as well. In our case our sites analytics script overrode global URL variable with its own value and after the update when axios tries to use URL, it throws URL is not a constructor error. This is unfortunate, but I would not blame axios for this issue.
author: TeemuKurki

body: Current v1.x has the browser environment guard and origin fallback in place. The remaining known cases in the thread point to either unsupported IE usage or another script replacing the global `URL` constructor, which Axios cannot safely work around. I am going to close this as not an Axios bug on the current v1.x.

author: AdarshNeoIto

body:
Thank you for raising this issue and sharing your insights! Let me address the situation:

### Acknowledgment of the Issue: 
The issue arises because, starting with Axios v1.7.8, the type definitions for HTTP methods (e.g., post, get) were updated to use axios.AxiosResponse instead of the previous global AxiosResponse. This change inadvertently causes TypeScript to infer deeply nested module paths (../../../../../node_modules/...) unless the return type is explicitly annotated or axios is imported.

### Root Cause: 
This behavior is not a bug but a side effect of the updated type definitions and how TypeScript handles type inference. When return types are not explicitly annotated, TypeScript tries to infer them, and in some cases, it resolves them in a non-portable way.

### Why This Matters: 
While it's possible to fix the issue by explicitly importing AxiosResponse or defining a custom response type, as you've correctly noted, this extra step introduces friction. This was not required in previous Axios versions, so it's understandable that this feels like an unnecessary regression in developer experience.

### Proposed Solution/Request: 
It might be helpful if Axios could:

Provide a default AxiosResponse type alias (as a global export) to avoid the need for nested imports.
Revisit the change in how types are defined to maintain backward compatibility with TypeScript's type inference.
Workaround in the Meantime: For users encountering this issue, a temporary workaround is to centralize the import of AxiosResponse or use a custom helper type, which avoids the need to import axios in every file.

```ts
import { AxiosResponse } from 'axios';

export type APIResponse<T = any> = AxiosResponse<T>;
```
### Community Feedback: 
I'd recommend discussing whether this change was intentional or an oversight and if there's scope for improvement in future releases.

Thank you again for your hard work on maintaining Axios! We understand the complexity of balancing feature updates with backward compatibility, and your efforts are greatly appreciated.

Hi @TQLeung , 

Thank you for submitting the issue. Just to be clear, I am not an official contributor to this open source project. I was interested in this bug and did some testing to try to reproduce this bug.

I was not **able to reproduce this bug**. Here is what I did to reproduce this bug.

1. Created a [REST API ](https://14a16e90d8f949ca8c9e1243e18a4a97.api.mockbin.io/)that returns a 400 error code. The REST API is publicly available without any AuthN/AuthZ credentials needed. The response data is { validationError: 'First Name Field not provided' }"

2. Created a sample code using node js 20 to recreate the bug  with axios version V 0.27.2.

```async function fireRequestGit() {
  service.interceptors.response.use(
    (res) => {
      console.log(res);
    },
    (error) => {
      console.log('error', error.response.data);
      return Promise.reject(error);
    }
  );

  service.get('/')
    .then((response) => {
      console.log(response);
    })
    .catch((error) => {
      console.error(error);
    });
}

fireRequestGit();
```



3. I ran the code and saw that axios returned the 400 error back correctly with the response data. The response service interceptor printed the error correctly. body: Hello, I've investigated this issue and my findings suggest that this is not a bug in Axios. Method: I used an Axios request interceptor to log the exact URL being sent, just before it leaves the client. Result: The logged URL was correctly formed, even when it contained the word "post". It did not have the extra trailing slash. Conclusion: This strongly indicates that the extra slash is being added by a server-side redirect (like a 301 or 302 response) after the request is sent. The problem lies in the server's configuration, not the Axios library. I believe this issue can be closed.

> (Casting no judgement or commentary on the motivations of axios or buzzoid )

in case this is news to anyone, open source sponsorship has long LONG been an SEO backlink technique.
author: bmuenzenmeyer

body:
on this we are not really "combing" through our sponsors list, if open collective accepts the sponsorship we accept it and make good on the agreement in the sponsor blocks provided for the tier.

as for if it is "ethical" i cannot be the judge of that. what makes a ethical company is largely up for debate, and more than likely contested depending on culture etc. it would be far outside of the purview of a open source project or my role as a maintainer to make this determination.

in the end we intend to use the money given to be able to improve axios, provide hosting and pay for domains etc. without funding it can be very hard to ask people to just keep on freely giving their time with no reward

Spent a long time debugging this as had the same issue with upgrading (axios and posting formData with vitest and JSDOM), and eventually got an error relating to Blob and arrayBuffer. It seems like this isn't implemented for Blob in JSDOM https://github.com/jsdom/jsdom/issues/2555

Adding a polyfil for Blob (as suggested on the issue) fixed the issue for me.

Still don't know if this is an axios issue though, as native fetch doesn't have this issue - likely due to the blob method on the response being used.

**Upgrading to v - 1.8.1 solved this issue.**

Module not found: Error: Can't resolve 'crypto' in '\node_modules\axios\lib'
BREAKING CHANGE: webpack < 5 used to include polyfills for node.js core modules by default.
This is no longer the case. Verify if you need this module and configure a polyfill for it.

If you want to include a polyfill, you need to:
        - add a fallback 'resolve.fallback: { "crypto": require.resolve("crypto-browserify") }'
        - install 'crypto-browserify'
If you don't want to include a polyfill, you can use an empty module like this:
        resolve.fallback: { "crypto": false }

You are most likely using a version range to install axios: `axios@^1.7` this will mean that `1.8.x` is included in this version range and will be installed the next time that you install anything that depends on axios.
author: Roaders

body:
yeah there is a type mismatch between AxiosRequestConfig (constructor param) and AxiosDefaults (type of this.defaults)

- AxiosRequestConfig.headers: optional, can be RawAxiosRequestHeaders | AxiosHeaders
- AxiosDefaults.headers: required, always HeadersDefaults (with common, get, post, put, patch, delete, head)

this is intentional because axios normalises the headers in this.defaults to include method-specific default headers (e.g., common: `{ Accept: 'application/json' }`, post: `{ Content-Type: 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' }`, etc.).

author: jasonsaayman

body:
By looking at the [fix](https://github.com/axios/axios/pull/6810/files), I think what's happening here is that if the config item `allowAbsoluteUrls` is not explicitly set to `false`, the [condition](https://github.com/axios/axios/blob/ec159e507bdf08c04ba1a10fe7710094e9e50ec9/lib/core/buildFullPath.js#L18) will be also false.
```
let allowAbsoluteUrls // undefined
console.log(allowAbsoluteUrls == false) // false
```
so the `baseURL` and `requestedURL` won't be combined still allowing potential SSRF

author: mhassan1

body:
Hey all,
I'm analyzing a Nuxt-based application that uses Axios v0.21.4, confirmed via the bundle at /_nuxt/4f15034.js. I understand this version is affected by:
[CVE-2023-45857] (CSRF via follow-redirects)
[CVE-2025-27152] / SNYK-JS-AXIOS-9292519 (SSRF via absolute URLs)
While reverse engineering the minified client bundle, I didn’t find any direct usage of axios.create()  instead, request building seems to be handled manually or via custom wrappers. Notably, I did find repeated usage of:

`new URL("https://a@b")
new Request(input, init)
fetch(url, options)
`
There are patterns where user-controllable values (like ref, pageURL, imageName, etc.) are interpolated into query strings and passed into URL() or fetchy() like so:
`const query = new URLSearchParams({
  pageURL: user_input,
  ref: external_link,
  ...
});

fetch(`/previews/s?${query}`, { method: "POST" });
`

The concern is that URL() constructor is being fed dynamic input, potentially allowing crafted URLs like http://attacker@internal.service  a classic case where SSRF can occur even without axios.create because redirect logic or follow-redirects gets invoked implicitly.
The presence of new URL("https://a@b"), .username, .password, and URLSearchParams logic implies that full absolute URLs could be constructed and sent to fetch() or wrapped into Request().
I attempted exploitation using OAST payloads via /previews/<x>/ping?ref=... and /previews/s?ref=..., but no callbacks yet. Still, this looks dangerously close to an SSRF vector, just obfuscated under a framework abstraction.

Can someone confirm:
Does this qualify as a reachable vector under the Axios advisory (since it leverages similar behavior)?
Could this still be vulnerable even without explicit use of axios.create()?
Has anyone reproduced exploitation using just new URL() + fetch() with tainted input?
Thanks in advance  trying to confirm whether this bundle warrants a report or is just a dead-end pattern.

body:
Yes, the **allowAbsoluteUrls** parameter is not part of AxiosRequestConfig. It is used internally in axios/lib/adapters/http.js to call buildFullPath in axios/lib/core/buildFullPath.js. Modifying its value to false is complex due to how it's integrated into the configuration.

I tried setting it to false, as they suggested in issue **6816**  https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/6816


but I'm facing issues in the test cases, and mocking this behavior is quite challenging. The error message I'm seeing is:

TypeError: URL is not a constructor

javascript
Copy
  > 1 | import axios, {
      | ^  
    2 | AxiosProgressEvent,

This seems related to how Axios internally constructs URLs, and mocking this will likely require additional adjustments.

body:
Hi @theo-sim-dev  thanks for reaching out. You correctly understood my suggestion. I made the suggestion, as I realised in my axios-token-manager npm module, I was using this approach as it very neatly allows a retry, when your code only has a instance of axios to use and due to it being an error intercepted does not directly know the http verb used in the request. 

However this use case had not been mentioned explicitly in the docs, and so I had to do some research and discovery, and thought it would be better if the docs made this explicit, rather than people having to work it out for themselves. As some may never do so and miss out on using it in this way. Hence my raising the suggestion in [this ticket](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/6846)

Tzinov15

body:
> This request does NOT work, errors with a Network Error, and does even show up in the network tab.

Of course, this is a problem with RN on Android as Axios is just a wrapper over the standard network API, and not a low-level native client for each client/mobile platform. This is a generic low-level network API error, so I suspect ReactNative is following the spec exactly, which states that a request with a body must have the `content-type` header. It's unlikely that Axisos can do anything about it.

https://github.com/facebook/react-native/issues/30176
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
>so I suspect ReactNative is following the spec exactly, which states that a request with a body must have the content-type header. 

Where is this documented? I know this is part of the `fetch` spec, but seemingly axios does not enforce this since said request works on iOS.
author: Tzinov15

body:
Technically, it is not written anywhere that it `must` be, the specification says it `should`. 
```
RFC 7231
A sender SHOULD generate a Content-Type header field in a request message that contains a payload body.
RFC 9110
If a message has a payload body, it should include a Content-Type header field describing the media type of that body.
```
But the request processing by the server is not guaranteed if it is not present. 
So, technically, the specification does not prohibit requiring its setting, which the RN developers may have done. However, it seems strange that RN behaves differently for Android and IOS, which probably indicates that the problem is in the native RN modules for the platform. RN appeared after the release of the fetch specifications, so it is not surprising that they could use its specification when designing the RN network stack. In addition, in some modern platforms, XMLHttpRequest is already emulated internally via fetch.
author: DigitalBrainJS
DigitalBrainJS

    );
```
on a `react-native` = `.73.6`, `expo` = `50.0.8` version of an app that _works_, even though I would imagine under the hood `rn-fetch-blob` is using the same internal android networking layers. 

looking at the history / git blame on that code inside NetworkingModule.java, that check for throwing an error for a request with no content type has been there seemingly for a long time

body:
You import two different Axios modules - esm and commonjs, and accordingly, two different AxiosHeaders classes that know nothing about each other. This will also happen when you have more than one version of the Axios package in the project and mix AxiosHeaders classes. In principle, if we consider it a bug, the support for this can be easily added, however, I don't think users often encounter this in practice.

As a workaround for now, you can use the toJSON() method
```js
new AxiosHeaders(new axios.AxiosHeaders().toJSON());
```
author: DigitalBrainJS

body:
Thanks for quick reply. Much appreciated.

You are exactly right. The class is bundle twice. It always happens for a typescript project with a 'aws4-axios' and 'axios'. dependencies 'aws4-axios' compiled to use 'require' (cjs) and if you use import 'axios' in your project it will use ESM. 
So 
1. axios is bundled twice. esbuild include cjs and esm versions of axios. it inflates bundle for AWS Lambdas 
2. if you pass custom empty headers (ESM) to the aws4 interceptor => you get the error.

P1. always happens
P2. happens less often.

Thank for your help

body:
Hi @alex-vukov 

Instead of only modifying a single attribute, we could support deep-copying an Axios instance and updating its properties through parameters at creation.

In this approach, the original and the cloned instance would initially share the same interceptors, but function as separate instances. This means new interceptors or properties could be changed in the clone without affecting the original.

A method like `clone(config)` would return a new instance with all configurations and interceptors copied, but without shared references, ensuring isolation between the original and the clone.


Other libraries implement similar functionality:

[resty - Client.Clone](https://pkg.go.dev/resty.dev/v3#Client.Clone) – See also: https://github.com/go-resty/resty/issues/773

[OkHttpClient.newBuilder()](https://square.github.io/okhttp/3.x/okhttp/okhttp3/OkHttpClient.html#:~:text=Customize%20your%20client%20with%20newBuilder)
author: WillianAgostini

body:
Hi @WillianAgostini,
This might not be very convenient in some cases. For example if you clone the axios instance but for some reason you have to add yet another interceptor you will have to add it to all cloned instances too. Removing is the same. You add it in one place but after the clone you have to remove it from multiple places. What I am looking for is a way to simply change the version of the API without touching the instance logic. I guess it's not very practical though. Maybe the best option would be to always concatenate it to the url as I am doing now.

Invalid use of Range header:
	•	Range: bytes=0-{totalSize} is incorrect, since totalSize is already the full length. Instead, use bytes=0- to request the full file.
	2.	onDownloadProgress does not work in Node.js:
	•	That callback only works in the browser. In Node.js, use response.data.on('data', ...) to track streaming progress.
	3.	Missing require("axios") in Node.js:
	•	Needed to import axios in a Node.js environment.

body: I am also experiencing this issue, randomly my network requests give "Network Error". I am using React-Native, if I run the application for the first time in the simulator, it sometimes works fine, but when I refresh or apply a change to the code, all my requests after a hot refresh fall into the "Network Error" state. However, for some reason that I don't fully understand, if I start the https api I have with ngrok and give the ngrok address as the base api to axios, it works fine. It gives the same "Network Error" error for the packages I use in my project, for example "getstream/stream-video-js" with ngrok address activated: When ngrok is activated for our own API, only requests from the packages I use give "Nework Error" or "Aborted" errors. without ngrok address: Here, requests from our own API give "Network Error"
author: ertucaglar

body: > I am also experiencing this issue, randomly my network requests give "Network Error". > > I am using React-Native, if I run the application for the first time in the simulator, it sometimes works fine, but when I refresh or apply a change to the code, all my requests after a hot refresh fall into the "Network Error" state. > > However, for some reason that I don't fully understand, if I start the https api I have with ngrok and give the ngrok address as the base api to axios, it works fine. > > It gives the same "Network Error" error for the packages I use in my project, for example "getstream/stream-video-js" > > with ngrok address activated: When ngrok is activated for our own API, only requests from the packages I use give "Nework Error" or "Aborted" errors. > > without ngrok address: Here, requests from our own API give "Network Error" > > Hi @ertucaglar I don't think the problem is on the Axios side Axios is just a wrapper around `fetch` or `XMLHttpRequest` in React Native. It doesn't handle the network requests directly — it relies on the JS engine and the underlying OS (iOS in this case) to perform the actual network operations. If your API works fine when accessed through ngrok but fails with your original domain, it's most likely a network or SSL configuration issue, not an Axios problem. React Native's Hot Refresh often breaks the networking context, caches, or the bridge between JS and native code. This has been known to cause similar issues with many other libraries, not just Axios. Other libraries (like `getstream/stream-video-js`) are also throwing the same "Network Error" or "Aborted" issues, which suggests the problem is more general and not specific to Axios. Take a look at this comment of mine ([Link](https://github.com/axios/axios/issues/6903#issuecomment-2844587587))

body:
I’ve also been experiencing increasing issues with network errors. In my case, an outdated AAAA DNS record was causing intermittent TLS handshake failures due to unsupported IPv6 connections—particularly when users were on cellular networks with unstable connectivity or frequent network switching.

I mitigated most of the issues by removing the AAAA record from my DNS configuration, which forces IPv6 resolution to fail fast and fall back to IPv4.

At this point, the issue seems to persist only on iOS 18.4 devices. I also believe this isn’t directly related to Axios, but rather due to a lower-level networking or protocol handling problem that may not be properly supported in the underlying stack.

body:
I just wanted to comment that I believe this issue isn’t related to Axios, but rather a broader problem specific to Safari. For example, even without using the simulator, I’ve encountered cases where network requests randomly show as “failed” or “lost.” It’s not exactly a CORS access error like the one seen here, but the behavior only occurs in Safari (both on mobile, in the simulator, and on macOS)

Our SPA clients report that their actions (requiring API POST/GET) requests sometimes fail unpredictably. I’ve been able to reproduce this consistently, but only in Safari. Notably, Safari version 17.0 appears to be unaffected.

This might be related to the issue discussed here:
https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/777999?page=4#852434022
author: MiguelQueiroz

body:
I am also experiencing this issue in my production environment, though it's very hard to reproduce. 

I have screen recordings where this error just trivially occurs, although we had some brief luck reproducing the error by locking the phone around the time the app makes an axios GET request.

The only thing I can really say is it happens consistently when there are multiple network requests pending and it seems that Safari on iOS and Mac is not liking it :/

Edit:

From reading about other people's experiences with keep-alive connections, I will see if returning a `Connection: close` will solve for now...
author: eastwood

body: ^ If anybody has tried this without success though, would greatly appreciate the heads up :)
author: eastwood

body:
On Apache, disabling keep-alive with this .htaccess rule resolves the issue:

`BrowserMatch "Safari" nokeepalive`

This is expected, Safari fails to retry after reusing a closed keep-alive connection.

They argue that RFC specifies retries only for GET requests since they are idempotent. I think this should be revisited: if the first request never reached the server, retrying a POST is harmless and does not cause duplicate work.

Additionally, GET requests with Content-Type: application/json trigger a CORS preflight. When the OPTIONS request fails (because it is not retried), the preflight fails and the GET request fails too.

And the fact that this changed suddendly after safari 18+ and only safari, its a huge impact.

Have issue open in Apple https://developer.apple.com/forums/thread/796906
author: MiguelQueiroz

body: @MiguelQueiroz Thanks, except what if a website is proxied through Cloudflare? They disallow setting the connection header, and we can't move away from them

body:
Closing, this is a WebKit-side regression affecting fetch/XHR on iOS 18.3.x, not an axios bug. Multiple reports against other HTTP clients (fetch directly, other libraries) surfaced in the same timeframe.

If you can produce a minimal repro that fails in axios but succeeds with raw fetch() on the same iOS version, please open a new issue and tag me.

body:
> I fixed it, it seems like this is the safest way while we wait for React Native's solution
> 
> ```
> config.resolver.resolveRequest = function packageExportsResolver(
>   context,
>   moduleImport,
>   platform
> ) {
>   // Use the browser version of the package for React Native
>   if (moduleImport === "axios" || moduleImport.startsWith("axios/")) {
>     return context.resolveRequest(
>       {
>         ...context,
>         unstable_conditionNames: ["browser"],
>       },
>       moduleImport,
>       platform
>     );
>   }
> 
>   // Fall back to normal resolution
>   return context.resolveRequest(context, moduleImport, platform);
> };
> ```

Just to be clear, the current behavior is the way React Native will be resolving modules going forward. The next steps are working with libraries such as this one to smooth out the package exports.

body:
Hi,

It appears that the problem you're encountering is related to requests to domains behind Cloudflare. After a fresh build, Axios works fine, but on subsequent app launches, the requests fail with a "Network Error."

This issue might be related to how Cloudflare handles caching or request routing. We recommend trying the following potential solutions:

1 - **Clear Cache**: Try clearing the cache in your React Native app, as it might be storing outdated request data. There are libraries like `react-native-clear-cache` that could help with this.

2 - **Update Axios**: Make sure you're using the latest version of Axios, as newer versions often fix issues and improve compatibility. You can update Axios by running:

```bash
npm install axios@latest
```

3 - **Retry Logic**: You can implement retry logic in your Axios requests to handle intermittent network errors. This can be done using libraries like `axios-retry`.

4 - **Cloudflare Configuration**: Check your Cloudflare settings to ensure that they are not interfering with subsequent requests. Specifically, verify the caching, firewall, and CORS settings to ensure they are configured correctly.


Feel free to adjust the details according to your needs!
author: inject3r

body:
**Update: Issue Resolved**

The issue appears to be related to the iOS Simulator (version 18.4) and not Axios itself or physical devices. After reading this related discussion on the React Native repository: [facebook/react-native#50510](https://github.com/facebook/react-native/issues/50510), I downgraded the iOS Simulator to version 18.2. After doing so, the problem was resolved.

So to clarify: this is not an Axios bug, but rather an iOS 18.4 Simulator-specific issue.

Thanks for your time — and special thanks to @inject3r for thinking along and helping out!
author: MitchellVermaningSouthfields

body:
## Root cause found — traced to OkHttp callTimeout

We hit this exact bug in a React Native app behind Cloudflare Tunnel.
Here's the full trace:

**The chain:**
1. `axios` sets `request.timeout = _config.timeout` (xhr adapter, line ~2796)
2. RN's `XMLHttpRequest.js` passes `this.timeout` to `RCTNetworking.sendRequest`
3. `NetworkingModule.java` converts it to OkHttp `callTimeout`
4. OkHttp fires `call.cancel()` → throws `IOException("Canceled")` (NOT `SocketTimeoutException`)
5. `ResponseUtil.java` only checks for `SocketTimeoutException` → reports as generic error
6. RN dispatches `error` event (not `timeout`) → axios reports `ERR_NETWORK`

**Why fetch works:** `whatwg-fetch` (RN's fetch polyfill) never sets `xhr.timeout`,
so OkHttp keeps its default unlimited timeouts and retries stale connections cleanly.

**Why it breaks on subsequent launches:** OkHttp's connection pool persists between
requests. When the app is backgrounded and reopened, keep-alive connections to
Cloudflare are stale. With `callTimeout=10s`, there's not enough time for
DNS+TLS+handshake on a new connection. Without it, OkHttp retries cleanly.

---

**Fix (one line):**
```js
const api = axios.create({
  adapter: 'fetch', // bypass XHR adapter, avoids OkHttp callTimeout bug
  timeout: 30000,
});
```

This uses axios's built-in fetch adapter (AbortController + JS setTimeout)
instead of XHR, avoiding the OkHttp callTimeout entirely.
All axios features (interceptors, transformers, etc.) still work.

**Alternative patch** for those who need the XHR adapter:
Replace `request.timeout = _config.timeout` with a JS-level `setTimeout`
that calls `request.abort()` — preserving timeout semantics without touching
OkHttp callTimeout. Full patch available on request.

Tested on: React Native 0.79, axios 1.15.2, Android 13, Cloudflare Tunnel.

author: DenisLopatin

body:
Well, this is how it is intended in the current Axios design, otherwise, the interceptors would not have complete data, for example, to simply retry the request or get access to the native request object for some reason. Besides, it is impossible to automatically 100% predict what is sensitive information, since such information can be transmitted not only in the standard authorization header. The level of logging and control of potential data leaks within the process is not the task of Axios or another end library, but the responsibility of a properly configured logging system and its subsystem for removing sensitive information before transferring it to an external storage system, if this is required by the security standards of the project, however, in such projects the logging system is carefully configured, and not simply left with default config to preserve all information as is, including non-standard enumerable props.
As was said earlier in the previous discussion, the maximum that can be done from the Axios side in the context of the current architecture is to make the meta information in the Axios error non-enumerable, although this can be risky within the current major version, due to introducing potential minor breaking changes. 
In any case, if a logging system logs the entire error object by default, the Axios error can be easily identified to remove the extra properties before passing it to the logging system, so this doesn't seem like a big problem.
author: DigitalBrainJS

body: I agree this is up to the developer to correctly configure their looging
author: jasonsaayman

body:
@DigitalBrainJS I understand the reasoning—putting responsibility on the developer and the logging system, and the need for interceptors to have full request context. Still, I believe this is a real problem.

There’s no other HTTP client I can think of that automatically leaks secrets like bearer tokens just by logging a 401 error with its stack trace. An error should not require sanitization before it's even safe to print. That's a dangerous default.

JavaScript error handling is already messy. Axios shouldn’t make it harder. That’s why I suggested a configuration option—not a change to the default behavior, but a way for developers to opt into stricter error sanitization. Yes, enabling it might break certain advanced use cases, but that tradeoff should be up to the developer.

Expecting logging systems to surgically filter out sensitive fields from Axios-specific errors isn't a clean or scalable solution. Having Axios optionally omit this information at the source would be a simple, additional line of defense in projects that care about minimizing risk.
